proposals.title: Centrifuge Investigation of Pile Downdrag and Soil-Structure Interaction in Deposits with Engineered Heterogeneity in Density and Permeability
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling of geotechnical systems, such as pile foundations in liquefiable soils or fly ash run-out, has historically relied on the simplifying assumption of uniform soil properties. This assumption is a known limitation, as natural and industrial deposits exhibit inherent spatial variability in density and permeability, which critically influences pore pressure dissipation, liquefaction triggering, and subsequent soil deformation. A systematic study of these effects is needed to assess the conservatism or risk in current design practices derived from uniform model tests.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing controlled, layered heterogeneity in density and permeability within centrifuge models will significantly alter the development of excess pore pressures, the pattern of soil deformation, and the resulting downdrag loads on piles compared to uniform deposit benchmarks. The impact will be non-linear and dependent on the sequence and contrast of the layers.
proposals.method: The research will employ a series of centrifuge tests using the novel, instrumented container developed by Kutter et al. for studying flow failures. Models will be constructed using Nevada sand and/or fly ash, with properties characterized via CPT and permeability tests. Heterogeneity will be introduced by creating distinct layers of controlled relative density (e.g., loose over dense, dense over loose) and by mixing fines to vary permeability. Instrumented model piles will measure axial load and bending moment. Tests will subject these stratified deposits to base shaking to induce liquefaction, with high-speed imaging and PIV analysis quantifying soil displacement fields. Results will be directly compared to prior uniform deposit tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first centrifuge-based dataset on pile response in systematically heterogeneous liquefiable deposits, quantifying the effects of layering on downdrag forces and soil flow mechanisms. It will deliver practical insights for refining analytical and numerical models that currently assume uniformity, leading to more risk-informed design frameworks for foundations in variable ground conditions.
proposals.novelty: While soil variability is acknowledged, its deliberate, controlled incorporation into dynamic centrifuge models of pile-soil interaction, specifically linking engineered stratigraphy to measured pile downdrag, represents a significant advancement beyond the standard uniform soil paradigm prevalent in physical modeling before 2026.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Validation of the Generalized Scaling Law for Pile Downdrag in Layered and Sloping Liquefiable Deposits
proposals.motivation: The Generalized Scaling Law (GSL) has shown promise for modeling soil-structure interaction in uniform liquefiable deposits using centrifuge tests. However, real-world soil profiles are often layered or sloping, introducing complex stress and drainage conditions. Validating the GSL for these more complex geologies is critical for extending its use in the design of piles and foundations against liquefaction-induced downdrag and lateral spreading.
proposals.hypothesis: The Generalized Scaling Law can accurately model the time-dependent downdrag loads and settlement behavior of single piles embedded in layered (e.g., non-liquefiable crust over liquefiable sand) and gently sloping liquefiable soil profiles, provided the scaling relationships correctly account for the evolving pore pressure dissipation and shear strain patterns in these heterogeneous systems.
proposals.method: A series of centrifuge tests at 50-g will be conducted using the novel, instrumented container developed for fly-ash run-out studies, adapted for controlled pile loading. Tests will model: (1) a two-layer system (silt crust over loose saturated sand), and (2) a uniform sloping deposit of liquefiable sand. A single instrumented aluminum pile will be installed. Liquefaction will be triggered by in-flight shaking. Pore pressures, pile strain (for downdrag load), and soil settlement will be measured. Prototype-scale behavior will be predicted using the GSL framework, and these predictions will be directly compared to the scaled centrifuge measurements to assess the law's accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental validation of the GSL for non-uniform soil profiles, a necessary step for its adoption in practice. It will yield quantitative data on downdrag loads in layered/sloping sites, directly informing seismic pile design. The results will also clarify the limitations of the GSL regarding boundary effects and complex pore pressure redistribution.
proposals.novelty: While the GSL has been tested for uniform deposits, its application to layered and sloping ground—where drainage conditions and failure mechanisms are fundamentally different—remains unexplored. This study uniquely combines advanced centrifuge modeling of these complex profiles with a focused validation of a fundamental scaling methodology.

proposals.title: Validation of the Generalized Scaling Law for Centrifuge Modeling of Pile Downdrag in Cohesive and Non-Plastic Silty Soils
proposals.motivation: The Generalized Scaling Law (GSL) for centrifuge modeling, which accounts for viscous and diffusion scaling conflicts in saturated soils, has been validated primarily for clean sands and uniform liquefiable deposits. However, its applicability to a broader range of geotechnical materials, particularly soils with significant fines content and plasticity, remains untested. This gap limits the confident use of centrifuge modeling for critical infrastructure problems involving pile foundations in mixed or layered soil profiles, which are common in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: The Generalized Scaling Law provides accurate scaling for centrifuge modeling of consolidation-induced downdrag on piles in soils with varying fines content and plasticity, provided the scaling laws for permeability and consolidation are correctly matched to the soil's index properties and stress-dependent behavior.
proposals.method: A series of centrifuge tests will be conducted using the researcher's proven instrumentation and container systems. Tests will model a single pile in a saturated deposit subjected to surface loading to induce consolidation and downdrag. The soil matrix will be systematically varied: from clean Ottawa sand, to non-plastic silts (e.g., silica flour), to low-plasticity clays (e.g., kaolin). Each test will be designed using the GSL framework, with careful scaling of permeability (k) and coefficient of consolidation (cv). Pore pressure dissipation, pile settlement, and downdrag load will be measured and compared against GSL-predicted prototype behavior and, where possible, analytical solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental dataset validating the GSL for soils beyond clean sands, directly informing the centrifuge modeling community on scaling procedures for silty and clayey soils. It will yield practical guidance for modeling pile-soil interaction in realistic, heterogeneous deposits, enhancing the reliability of physical models for foundation design in challenging ground conditions.
proposals.novelty: While the GSL is established in principle, its experimental validation has been confined to liquefiable sands; this study explicitly tests its limits by applying it to cohesive and non-plastic fines, a critical step for generalizing the law. The integration of the researcher's expertise in pile downdrag and novel centrifuge container design with this fundamental scaling question creates a unique synthesis.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Investigation of Slope Inclination Effects on Liquefaction-Induced Flow Failure and Pile Downdrag
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical practice for assessing liquefaction-induced lateral spreading and pile downdrag often relies on empirical models calibrated against case histories and centrifuge tests, predominantly on gentle slopes near 5 degrees. This creates a significant knowledge gap for infrastructure on steeper or more variable natural and man-made slopes, such as embankment dams, tailings storage facilities, and sloping alluvial deposits. A systematic understanding of failure mechanisms across a broader inclination spectrum is critical for risk assessment and resilient design.
proposals.hypothesis: The transition from limited lateral deformation to catastrophic flow failure in liquefiable deposits, and the resulting downdrag loads on embedded piles, is governed by a non-linear relationship with slope angle, with critical thresholds existing beyond the commonly studied 5-degree condition.
proposals.method: The research will employ a series of centrifuge tests using the novel, instrumented container developed by the PI for flow studies. Uniform and layered deposits of saturated, liquefiable silica sand and non-plastic silt will be constructed at inclinations of 0°, 2.5°, 5°, 10°, and 15°. Each model will be instrumented with pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement markers. Pile groups instrumented with strain gauges will be installed in select models. Models will be spun to 50-g and subjected to a suite of scaled earthquake motions. Data on triggering, flow run-out distance, shear strain localization, and pile bending moments/drag loads will be quantified.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unique dataset mapping liquefaction response and pile performance from flat ground to steep slopes. It will provide empirical scaling relationships between slope angle, triggering intensity, flow displacement, and structural demand. The findings will directly inform the revision of guidelines for slope-specific liquefaction hazard evaluation and the design of pile foundations in sloping liquefiable ground.
proposals.novelty: While previous centrifuge studies have examined lateral spreading at ~5 degrees or the run-out of failed slopes, this work systematically decouples and investigates the *initiation* and *consequences* of liquefaction flow failure as a direct function of initial slope angle, filling a key parametric gap in the experimental database.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Pile Downdrag and Settlement in Layered, Liquefiable Soils Under Complex Seismic Motions
proposals.motivation: Current design codes and centrifuge studies for liquefaction-induced downdrag often assume uniform soil deposits, yet real-world sites are frequently layered with varying permeability and strength. Furthermore, the influence of multi-directional or long-duration ground motions on the evolution of negative skin friction and post-liquefaction settlement in such complex stratigraphies is poorly understood. Validating numerical models and design methods for these realistic conditions is a critical gap in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The magnitude and distribution of downdrag loads and pile settlement in layered liquefiable deposits are significantly influenced by the interaction between soil layer properties and the frequency content/duration of the input ground motion, leading to design loads that differ from those predicted for uniform deposits.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of centrifuge tests at 50-g using a novel, instrumented container capable of simulating complex stratigraphies. Experiments will feature a saturated, layered model comprising a non-liquefiable crust over a loose, liquefiable silt layer (e.g., silica flour) atop a dense sand base. Instrumented aluminum model piles will be installed. A suite of earthquake motions (e.g., scaled recordings from the 1995 Kobe and 2011 Christchurch earthquakes) with varying characteristics will be applied via a servo-hydraulic shaker. Key measurements will include pore pressures at multiple depths, pile strain (for bending moment and axial load), and soil settlement using PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unique, high-quality dataset of pile response in layered, liquefiable systems under complex shaking, directly serving as a benchmark for validating advanced numerical tools (e.g., PM4Silt, OpenSees). It will provide quantitative insights into how layering affects the timing and magnitude of downdrag forces, leading to more rational and potentially economical pile design recommendations for stratified sites.
proposals.novelty: While Kutter's prior work focused on uniform deposits, this proposal systematically integrates three complexities—soil layering, realistic ground motion variety, and detailed run-out/settlement measurement—into a single experimental framework to study their coupled effects on pile performance, a step beyond current state-of-the-art centrifuge testing.

proposals.title: A Formal Verification and Validation Framework for Geotechnical Centrifuge Modeling: Application to Pile Downdrag in Liquefiable Soils
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge modeling is a critical tool for validating numerical models and informing design, yet its validation practices are often ad-hoc and qualitative. This contrasts with mature fields like Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), which employ rigorous, hierarchical Verification and Validation (V&V) procedures to quantify numerical and modeling errors. Adopting such a formal framework is essential to improve the credibility and predictive capability of geotechnical simulations, particularly for complex, rate-dependent phenomena like liquefaction-induced flow and pile downdrag.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured V&V hierarchy, adapted from CFD standards (e.g., ASME V&V 20), to geotechnical centrifuge experiments will systematically quantify experimental uncertainty and provide a robust benchmark for validating advanced constitutive models in numerical simulations of pile-soil interaction.
proposals.method: The research will implement the CFD V&V hierarchy, defining Verification (solving equations correctly) and Validation (solving correct equations) stages for geotechnical problems. We will use B. Kutter's existing centrifuge data on pile downdrag in liquefiable deposits as the validation benchmark. The method involves: 1) Quantifying experimental uncertainty via repeated tests and detailed error analysis of sensors and boundary conditions. 2) Performing Code Verification of a selected numerical platform (e.g., OpenSees, FLAC) using manufactured analytical solutions. 3) Conducting Solution Verification via grid convergence studies for the pile-soil system. 4) Executing Validation by comparing numerical predictions against the quantified centrifuge data, calculating validation metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a formal, transferable protocol for geotechnical model validation, moving the field toward more standardized and transparent practices. It will deliver a rigorously validated numerical model for liquefaction-induced downdrag, directly improving pile design reliability. The framework will set a precedent for validating simulations of other complex geotechnical hazards.
proposals.novelty: While V&V is standard in CFD, its formal application to the validation of geotechnical centrifuge modeling—treating the physical experiment as the benchmark within a quantified uncertainty framework—is novel. This bridges a significant methodological gap between computational and experimental geomechanics.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Validation Framework for Pile Downdrag in Liquefiable Soils: Calibrating Constitutive Models via Direct Simple Shear for Centrifuge Prediction
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of liquefaction-induced downdrag and settlement on piles is critical for resilient foundation design. While centrifuge modeling provides invaluable system-level validation, its high cost and complexity limit rapid iteration and model calibration. A robust, multi-scale framework linking element-level soil behavior to full physical models is needed to improve the reliability and efficiency of geotechnical predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: Constitutive model parameters for fly ash and liquefiable sands, calibrated against stress-path-controlled direct simple shear (DSS) tests, will enable accurate a priori numerical prediction of centrifuge-measured pile downdrag and soil run-out behavior.
proposals.method: The research will employ a staged experimental and numerical workflow. First, a comprehensive suite of stress-path-controlled DSS tests will be performed on the target materials (fly ash, uniform sand) to characterize cyclic strength, post-liquefaction shear strain accumulation, and volumetric settlement. Data from these tests will be used to calibrate critical state-based constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) within a finite element framework. The calibrated models will then be used to perform blind numerical simulations of Kutter's prior centrifuge tests on pile downdrag and fly ash run-out. The final step involves direct validation by comparing these blind numerical predictions against the extensive existing centrifuge dataset of pile loads, pore pressures, and displacements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated, practical pathway for using element tests to reliably scale up to complex boundary value problems. It will provide rigorously calibrated constitutive parameters for challenging materials like fly ash, directly benefiting numerical practice in liquefaction analysis. The framework will enhance the interpretative value of future centrifuge tests by separating model uncertainty from systemic experimental effects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, blind coupling of DSS-calibrated models to Kutter's unique centrifuge experiments on both pile downdrag and fly ash flow, creating a closed-loop validation for constitutive models under truly prototype stress conditions. This moves beyond typical element-to-numerical model comparisons by anchoring the entire process in high-quality physical system-level data.

proposals.title: A Numerical Validation Framework for Centrifuge Scaling Laws: Application to Pile Downdrag in Liquefiable Soils
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical physical modeling, relying on scaling laws to relate model behavior to prototype-scale phenomena. However, direct validation of these scaling laws is challenging, as creating a true, full-scale prototype for comparison is often impractical. This research addresses the 'dual problem' by using a high-fidelity numerical model as a virtual prototype to rigorously test the validity of centrifuge scaling for complex soil-structure interaction problems, such as liquefaction-induced downdrag on piles.
proposals.hypothesis: A validated numerical constitutive model, when used to simulate both a full-scale prototype and its centrifuge-scaled counterpart under equivalent gravity fields, will produce consistent results for key engineering parameters (e.g., pile settlement, bending moment, excess pore pressure), thereby confirming the fidelity of centrifuge scaling laws for this class of problems.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-stage numerical modeling approach using a finite difference or finite element platform (e.g., FLAC or OpenSees). First, a constitutive model (such as PM4Sand or a critical state model) will be calibrated against existing centrifuge test data from Kutter's prior work on pile downdrag. This validated model will then be used to simulate two scenarios: a full-scale prototype under 1g, and a geometrically scaled model under Ng (where N is the centrifuge scaling factor). Identical soil parameters and loading sequences will be applied. The time-scaling of dynamic events will be explicitly enforced. The outputs—pile response and soil liquefaction patterns—will be compared quantitatively.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, cost-effective numerical framework for validating centrifuge scaling laws, moving beyond traditional empirical comparisons. It will deliver specific insights into the accuracy of scaling for transient liquefaction and downdrag mechanisms. The resulting methodology will be a valuable tool for designing and interpreting future centrifuge tests, increasing confidence in extrapolating model results to field-scale predictions.
proposals.novelty: While numerical models are often calibrated to centrifuge data, systematically using a single, validated numerical model to act as *both* the prototype and the centrifuge model to directly test scaling law validity is a non-obvious inversion of the standard workflow. This approach isolates scaling effects from model uncertainty.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Validation of Liquefaction-Induced Pile Downdrag and Settlement in Layered Soil Profiles for LEAP
proposals.motivation: The Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects (LEAP) have successfully benchmarked numerical models for uniform, clean sands, but natural soil deposits are often layered or non-uniform. The behavior of pile foundations during and after liquefaction in such complex profiles, particularly the downdrag loads and differential settlements, remains poorly validated. This gap limits the confident application of LEAP-derived insights to real-world geotechnical design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of low-permeability or fine-grained layers within a liquefiable deposit will significantly alter the dissipation of excess pore pressures, thereby modifying the time-history and magnitude of liquefaction-induced downdrag loads and permanent pile settlements compared to uniform deposits.
proposals.method: The research will employ centrifuge modeling, building on Kutter's expertise and novel container designs. A series of tests will be conducted in the UC Davis centrifuge, instrumenting model piles in layered soil profiles (e.g., Ottawa F-65 sand interlayered with silt or a fine sand layer). A controlled liquefaction event will be triggered via base shaking. The experimental data (pore pressures, pile bending moments, settlements) will be shared as a new, open-access LEAP case history. Concurrently, the data will be used to validate advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) within finite element frameworks (e.g., OpenSees, FLAC) to assess their capability to capture the coupled hydromechanical response in layered systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-quality, publicly available centrifuge dataset on pile response in layered liquefiable soils for the LEAP community. It will advance the validation of numerical tools beyond uniform deposits, providing crucial data for calibrating models of partial drainage and void redistribution effects. The results will directly inform improved design guidelines for pile foundations in realistic, stratified soil conditions.
proposals.novelty: While LEAP has focused on uniform sands, this proposal systematically extends the validation framework to stratified deposits, explicitly linking soil fabric complexity to pile performance. It leverages novel centrifuge instrumentation to capture the transient mechanisms governing downdrag in layered systems, a scenario not yet addressed in prior LEAP phases.

proposals.title: A Benchmarking Study and Protocol for Centrifuge Modeling of Pile Downdrag in Liquefiable Soils: Towards Reduced Inter-Facility Scatter
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical physical modeling, yet significant scatter in results between different facilities persists, undermining confidence in quantitative predictions for critical problems like pile downdrag. This inconsistency, evident in prior liquefaction and fly-ash run-out studies, stems from non-standardized procedures for model preparation, instrumentation, and scaling of dynamic events. Standardized protocols are urgently needed to improve the reliability and transferability of centrifuge data for performance-based design.
proposals.hypothesis: That a significant portion of the observed inter-facility scatter in centrifuge test results for pile-soil interaction in liquefiable deposits can be eliminated by developing and implementing a standardized, detailed protocol for model construction, saturation, instrumentation placement, and seismic motion scaling.
proposals.method: The research will conduct a series of benchmark centrifuge tests at two facilities, replicating a canonical pile downdrag in liquefiable sand scenario. The core method involves a three-phase experimental design: 1) A baseline phase using each facility's current in-house methods to quantify existing scatter. 2) A development phase to create a detailed, step-by-step protocol addressing soil pluviation density control, viscous pore fluid preparation, piezometer tip saturation, and input motion scaling based on established scaling laws. 3) A validation phase where both facilities strictly adhere to the new protocol for replicate tests. Data analysis will focus on comparing key metrics: excess pore pressure generation, pile settlement, and bending moment profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first community-vetted, detailed procedural guideline for centrifuge modeling of pile-soil interaction in liquefiable soils, directly reducing experimental uncertainty. It will provide a quantitative assessment of how much scatter is attributable to procedural differences versus inherent soil variability. The resulting protocol will serve as a template for standardizing tests for other geotechnical hazards.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have noted inter-facility discrepancies, this work proactively develops and validates a corrective procedural standard, moving from problem identification to solution. It uniquely integrates lessons from both liquefaction-induced downdrag and flow failure (fly-ash run-out) modeling to create a robust protocol for dynamic centrifuge testing.

proposals.title: Calibration of Advanced Constitutive Models for Pile-Soil Interaction Under High-Amplitude Seismic Shaking Using Centrifuge Experiments
proposals.motivation: Current numerical models for predicting liquefaction-induced pile downdrag and soil-structure interaction are often calibrated against moderate shaking events. However, the performance of these models under stronger shaking, which can induce higher excess pore pressures, greater shear strains, and more pronounced non-linear soil behavior, remains inadequately validated. This gap limits the reliability of numerical tools for the design of deep foundations in regions of high seismicity, where extreme shaking scenarios must be considered.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) can accurately capture the cyclic stress-strain response and pore pressure generation in liquefiable deposits under high-amplitude base excitations, but their parameters require recalibration for this intense loading regime. The resulting calibrated models will provide more accurate predictions of pile settlement and bending moment for strong seismic events.
proposals.method: This research will conduct a series of centrifuge model tests using a novel, instrumented container to subject uniform, saturated liquefiable sand deposits with instrumented piles to a suite of high-amplitude base motions. The experimental data (accelerations, pore pressures, pile bending moments, settlements) will be used to calibrate and validate numerical models in a finite difference framework (e.g., FLAC). The calibration will focus on key parameters within the PM4Sand model, such as the contraction rate parameter and fabric-dilatancy constants, using an inverse analysis approach to optimize the fit between simulated and measured soil and pile response metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously calibrated set of constitutive model parameters for high-amplitude shaking, directly enhancing the predictive capability of numerical simulations for critical infrastructure design. It will yield a benchmark dataset of high-quality centrifuge measurements under strong shaking, valuable for future model development. The findings will inform revisions to design guidelines concerning the analysis of deep foundations subjected to extreme seismic loading.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge tests of pile liquefaction are common, systematic calibration of advanced constitutive models specifically for the high-amplitude shaking regime, using a novel container designed to minimize boundary effects, is a significant step forward. This directly addresses a known limitation in extrapolating model performance from moderate to strong shaking levels.

proposals.title: Calibration of Ottawa Sand Constitutive Model Parameters via High-Precision Cyclic Simple Shear Testing for Centrifuge Model Validation
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of soil-structure interaction, such as liquefaction-induced downdrag and settlement on piles, relies on constitutive models calibrated with high-quality element test data. Pre-2026 centrifuge modeling, as conducted by Kutter et al., often uses empirical correlations or back-analysis from model tests, introducing uncertainty. A direct, rigorous calibration of model parameters for standard sands like Ottawa F-65 from controlled cyclic simple shear tests is needed to enhance the predictive reliability of numerical simulations that complement physical modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: Constitutive model parameters (e.g., for PM4Sand or a similar critical state-based model) derived directly from high-precision cyclic simple shear tests on Ottawa sand will provide superior predictive capability for centrifuge model outcomes, particularly for liquefaction triggering and post-liquefaction deformation, compared to parameters estimated from index properties or standard penetration test correlations.
proposals.method: The research will employ a high-precision, computer-controlled cyclic simple shear device to conduct stress-controlled tests on reconstituted specimens of Ottawa F-65 sand at varying relative densities and confining stresses relevant to centrifuge models. Key cyclic strength curves and volumetric strain accumulation data will be measured. These experimental results will be used to calibrate the parameters of the PM4Sand constitutive model (version 3.1) via an inverse analysis optimization routine, systematically minimizing the error between test data and model simulations. The calibrated parameters will then be validated by simulating published centrifuge tests of pile downdrag in liquefiable deposits and comparing predicted and measured pile settlements and pore pressure responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmarked, directly calibrated parameter set for Ottawa sand, a widely used material in physical modeling, for critical state-based constitutive models. It will establish a robust, transparent methodology for linking element test data to centrifuge model performance, reducing epistemic uncertainty in numerical predictions. The results will directly benefit the design and interpretation of centrifuge experiments on pile foundations in liquefiable ground.
proposals.novelty: While simple shear tests and constitutive models are established, the novelty lies in the targeted, high-fidelity calibration for a specific, well-documented sand specifically to validate and enhance the interpretation of centrifuge physical models, creating a closed loop between element testing, numerical simulation, and physical modeling at the centrifuge scale.

proposals.title: Prototype-Scale Validation of Centrifuge-Derived Models for Lateral Flow and Downdrag on Piles in Sloping Liquefiable Deposits
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling has been a cornerstone for understanding soil-structure interaction during liquefaction, particularly for phenomena like lateral spreading and pile downdrag. However, a critical gap exists in validating these scaled physical models at full, prototype scale in the field. This validation is essential for improving the confidence and calibration of centrifuge-derived design methods, which are widely used in seismic geotechnical engineering for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: That the mechanisms and magnitudes of liquefaction-induced lateral flow and the resulting downdrag loads on pile foundations, as characterized in centrifuge models, can be accurately predicted and validated through controlled, full-scale field testing with engineered soil deposits and in-situ excitation.
proposals.method: The research will design and construct a large, instrumented, gently sloping test bed using a controlled, saturated, liquefiable material (e.g., clean sand or silt). A dense array of pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement sensors will be embedded. One or more instrumented model piles will be installed. Controlled excitation will be provided using a large, mobile vibroseis truck or explosive charges to induce liquefaction. Data on soil response, flow displacement, and pile loads will be captured and directly compared to predictions from prior centrifuge tests (like Kutter's 2015 work) using established scaling laws.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset directly linking centrifuge model behavior to a controlled, full-scale prototype for liquefaction-induced lateral deformation. It will yield crucial validation for scaling laws and centrifuge modeling techniques, directly informing the calibration of numerical models and improving the reliability of pile design in liquefiable slopes.
proposals.novelty: While field case histories exist, a controlled, fully instrumented, prototype-scale liquefaction test designed explicitly to validate centrifuge modeling results for soil-pile interaction in slopes is unprecedented. It bridges the critical gap between physical scale modeling and real-world implementation.

proposals.title: Inverse Calibration of Advanced Constitutive Model Parameters for Liquefiable Soils Using Centrifuge Test Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of soil-pile interaction during liquefaction is critical for seismic design, yet constitutive model parameters for advanced frameworks (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) are notoriously difficult to calibrate from conventional lab tests. Centrifuge tests provide rich, high-quality data on system response, including accelerations and pore pressures, which are currently underutilized for direct parameter estimation. An inverse method to back-calculate these parameters would bridge the gap between high-fidelity physical modeling and robust numerical simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: That the constitutive parameters of an advanced plasticity model for liquefiable soils can be uniquely and reliably estimated by inversely analyzing the dynamic acceleration and pore pressure response recorded in a centrifuge model test of a pile-foundation system.
proposals.method: The research will employ a Bayesian inverse analysis framework, specifically Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, to infer posterior distributions of key constitutive parameters (e.g., contraction rate parameter, fabric-dilatancy coefficients). A finite element model of the centrifuge test, using an open-source platform like OpenSees, will serve as the forward model. The misfit function will compare simulated and measured time histories of accelerations and pore pressures at multiple sensor locations from tests on uniform liquefiable deposits, leveraging Dr. Kutter's existing experimental data and novel container instrumentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorous, data-driven calibration protocol for advanced constitutive models, directly linking physical centrifuge observations to numerical parameters. It will enhance the reliability of numerical predictions for pile downdrag and soil-structure interaction in liquefiable ground. The methodology will be a template for validating and calibrating models for other challenging geomaterials, such as fly ash.
proposals.novelty: While inverse analysis is known in geomechanics, its application to directly back-calculate advanced constitutive parameters from full-system centrifuge response—integrating both acceleration and pore pressure data within a Bayesian framework—is a significant advancement over traditional trial-and-error or element-test-based calibration methods.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Liquefaction-Induced Flow and Pile Downdrag in Sloping Deposits with Spatially Variable Initial Density
proposals.motivation: Current centrifuge models for liquefaction-induced flow and pile downdrag often assume uniform initial soil density, which is a significant simplification of real field conditions where natural deposits exhibit spatial variability. This assumption may lead to inaccurate predictions of run-out distances, flow kinematics, and the magnitude and distribution of downdrag loads on pile foundations. Addressing this gap is critical for improving the reliability of seismic performance assessments for infrastructure on or within liquefiable slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: Inherent spatial variability in the initial relative density of a sloping liquefiable deposit will significantly alter the post-liquefaction flow behavior and the resulting downdrag loads on embedded piles compared to predictions from models assuming uniform density.
proposals.method: The research will employ centrifuge modeling using a novel instrumented container capable of simulating sloping ground. A uniform fine sand (e.g., Nevada Sand) will be pluviated using a traveling pluviator with a programmable path and flow rate to create a sloping deposit with controlled, spatially varying density zones (e.g., looser zones upslope, denser zones downslope). Instrumentation will include pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement markers within the soil, alongside strain-gauged model piles. A series of tests will be conducted, varying the density distribution pattern, and compared directly to baseline tests with uniform density. Data analysis will focus on correlating the initial density field with the triggering of liquefaction, subsequent flow deformation, and measured pile bending moments and downdrag forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic experimental dataset linking pre-liquefaction density heterogeneity to post-liquefaction flow and pile response. It will yield new insights into how natural variability controls failure mechanisms, leading to more nuanced design guidelines. The findings will directly inform the calibration and validation of advanced numerical models that attempt to incorporate spatial randomness.
proposals.novelty: While spatial variability is recognized in geostatistics, its deliberate, controlled incorporation into physical centrifuge models of liquefaction flow slides and pile interaction represents a significant advancement beyond the standard uniform density assumption prevalent in pre-2026 experimental practice.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Pile Downdrag and Settlement in Silty Sands and Non-Plastic Fines: Validation of Constitutive Models for Realistic Soil Profiles
proposals.motivation: Current design and analysis for pile foundations in liquefiable soils often rely on constitutive models calibrated for clean sands. However, natural and anthropogenic deposits (e.g., hydraulic fills, tailings, aged alluvium) frequently contain significant fines content, which profoundly alters cyclic strength, permeability, and post-liquefaction volumetric strain. This discrepancy leads to significant uncertainty in predicting liquefaction-induced downdrag and settlement, posing a risk to infrastructure. Validating models with realistic soil mixtures is therefore a critical step towards more reliable geotechnical design.
proposals.hypothesis: Constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, UBCSAND) calibrated solely on clean sand data will systematically over-predict or under-predict pore pressure generation, dissipation, and associated downdrag loads on piles when applied to soils with non-plastic fines. The inclusion of fines-specific parameters, such as state-dependent shear modulus and revised contraction/dilation rules, is necessary for accurate prediction.
proposals.method: The research will employ a series of centrifuge tests at the University of California, Davis, using the novel instrumented container from recent fly ash studies. We will create uniform and layered soil models using Ottawa sand mixed with non-plastic silt (e.g., crushed silica flour) at controlled fines contents (e.g., 10%, 25%). A single instrumented model pile will be installed. Tests will subject the models to controlled base shaking to trigger liquefaction. We will measure pore pressures, accelerations, pile bending moments, and settlement. The experimental results will be used to validate and refine numerical simulations conducted in OpenSees or FLAC, explicitly implementing existing constitutive models with and without fines-content modifiers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-quality, benchmark experimental dataset for pile response in silty, liquefiable soils, filling a key gap in centrifuge validation studies. It will deliver specific recommendations for modifying or selecting constitutive model parameters to account for fines content, directly improving the predictive capability of numerical tools used in practice. The findings will advance the design of pile foundations in prevalent non-clean sand deposits.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge testing of piles in clean sands is established, this proposal systematically inverts that assumption by focusing validation efforts explicitly on soil mixtures with fines, bridging the gap between idealized laboratory models and common field conditions. It leverages recent advancements in experimental container design to study the coupled soil-structure interaction problem in these under-characterized materials.

proposals.title: Validation of Pile Downdrag and Soil Flow Models Using a Novel Flexible-Boundary Centrifuge Container
proposals.motivation: Accurate centrifuge modeling of soil-structure interaction, particularly for pile downdrag in liquefiable soils or flow failures in materials like fly ash, is compromised by rigid container boundaries. These boundaries constrain soil deformation, generating non-physical shear stresses and arching effects that distort the measured loads on piles and the kinematics of soil flow. Validating numerical models with data from such tests can lead to unconservative or erroneous design predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: Using a flexible-wall container that minimizes lateral boundary constraint will produce centrifuge test data on pile downdrag and soil flow run-out that is in significantly closer agreement with validated, high-fidelity numerical models (e.g., coupled finite element analysis) than data from equivalent rigid-wall container tests.
proposals.method: The research will leverage the novel, instrumented flexible-wall container previously designed by the PI for fly ash run-out experiments. A series of centrifuge tests will be conducted, comparing pile response in liquefiable sand deposits and flow behavior of fly ash deposits under loss of confinement in both the flexible container and a standard rigid equivalent. The experimental data, including pile bending moments, axial load, soil accelerations, pore pressures, and high-speed deformation fields from PIV, will be used to validate two established numerical frameworks: a coupled effective-stress plasticity model (e.g., PM4Sand) for liquefaction-induced downdrag, and a rate-dependent constitutive model for fly ash flow. The validation will quantify the reduction in error metrics (e.g., pile load, run-out distance) when using flexible-boundary test data for model calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark dataset for validating numerical models of soil-structure interaction and flow landslides under minimally constrained boundary conditions. It will deliver quantitative guidance on the magnitude of error introduced by rigid boundaries in specific geotechnical problems. The outcome will strengthen confidence in using centrifuge data for high-stakes model calibration and improve the predictive capability of numerical tools for pile design in liquefiable ground and hazard assessment for impoundment failures.
proposals.novelty: While flexible containers exist, their systematic use for validating numerical models of pile downdrag and comparing the fidelity of data directly against rigid-container results is not well established. This proposal uniquely integrates the PI's recent container innovation with their expertise in pile liquefaction and fly ash flow to critically assess a core assumption in physical modeling.

proposals.title: Validation of Advanced Constitutive Models for Pile-Soil Interaction in Liquefiable Ground Using the LEAP Framework
proposals.motivation: The Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects (LEAP) have established a benchmark for validating numerical simulations of soil liquefaction against centrifuge test data. However, validation efforts have largely focused on relatively simple constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, UBCSAND). To improve the predictive capability for complex scenarios like pile downdrag and lateral spreading, it is critical to test more advanced models that incorporate fabric effects, anisotropic hardening, and cyclic degradation, which are not yet rigorously benchmarked within the LEAP framework.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced constitutive models (e.g., SANISAND, PM4Silt, or novel fabric-dependent models) will provide more accurate and reliable predictions of pore pressure generation, shear strain localization, and resulting pile settlements and loads in layered liquefiable deposits when validated against the high-quality, repeatable LEAP centrifuge datasets.
proposals.method: This research will apply the LEAP-2017 and LEAP-2022 validation protocols to selected advanced constitutive models implemented in platforms like OpenSees or FLAC. The centrifuge test data for uniform and sloping liquefiable deposits with pile groups will serve as the benchmark. The technical approach involves: 1) Calibrating model parameters against element test data where available, 2) Performing blind predictions of centrifuge-measured accelerations, pore pressures, displacements, and pile bending moments for specified validation phases, and 3) Conducting systematic sensitivity analyses to identify the influence of specific advanced features (e.g., fabric tensor, memory surface) on the predictive outcome. The novel centrifuge container design expertise from Kutter's prior fly ash work will inform assessment of boundary effects in the simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a rigorous, publicly available benchmark of advanced constitutive model performance for a critical class of geotechnical problems. It will provide practitioners with guidance on the necessity and implementation complexity of these models for design. Furthermore, it will identify specific gaps in model formulation that future constitutive development must address to improve simulation fidelity.
proposals.novelty: While LEAP has validated simplified models, a systematic evaluation of next-generation constitutive models within this controlled, multi-laboratory framework has not been conducted. The novelty lies in leveraging the unique, high-quality LEAP dataset to test the real-world predictive gain offered by these computationally complex models for boundary-value problems involving soil-pile interaction.

proposals.title: Inverse Calibration of Critical State Parameters for Fly Ash and Liquefiable Soils Using Centrifuge Downdrag and Run-out Data
proposals.motivation: Calibrating constitutive models for materials like fly ash and liquefiable sands for pile foundation design relies heavily on laboratory element tests, which may not capture the complex stress paths and boundary conditions of real field events. The discrepancy between element test parameters and full-scale system response leads to uncertainty in predicting phenomena like liquefaction-induced downdrag on piles or post-liquefaction flow. Using high-quality centrifuge data as a 'ground truth' offers a powerful but underutilized avenue to refine these fundamental parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: Critical state soil parameters (e.g., critical state line slope λ, intercept Γ, and state parameter ξ) back-calculated from centrifuge model tests of pile downdrag and flow slides will provide a more accurate and representative calibration for element test simulations than parameters derived from conventional direct simple shear or triaxial tests alone.
proposals.method: The research will employ an inverse analysis framework. First, high-resolution instrumentation data (pore pressures, displacements, loads) from existing centrifuge tests on pile downdrag in uniform deposits and fly ash run-out will be used as target outputs. A finite element model of the centrifuge experiment, using a critical state-based constitutive model (e.g., NorSand or MIT-SR), will be developed. An optimization algorithm, such as a Bayesian updating framework or a genetic algorithm, will then iteratively adjust the constitutive model's critical state parameters to minimize the error between the numerical simulation and the centrifuge-measured system response (e.g., pile settlement time-history or run-out distance).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated set of critical state parameters for fly ash and liquefiable soils explicitly calibrated for system-level performance, reducing epistemic uncertainty in numerical predictions. It will establish a robust methodology for using centrifuge data as a benchmark for constitutive model calibration, bridging the gap between element and system behavior in geotechnical practice.
proposals.novelty: While inverse analysis is known, its application to directly calibrate fundamental critical state parameters from complex centrifuge failure mechanisms (downdrag, flow) for use in simple element test simulations is novel. This approach inverts the traditional scaling paradigm, using the well-controlled physical model to inform the basic laboratory element characterization.

proposals.title: Inversion of Surface Deformation Data from Centrifuge Models to Infer Spatial Soil Stiffness and Density Profiles
proposals.motivation: Accurate characterization of subsurface soil properties, such as density and stiffness, is critical for predicting foundation settlement and pile downdrag, especially in problematic soils like fly ash or liquefiable deposits. Current in-situ testing is expensive and provides only point measurements, limiting the ability to capture spatial variability. This research addresses the inverse problem of inferring continuous subsurface property distributions from measured surface deformations, a more efficient and comprehensive approach for geotechnical site characterization.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution of soil density and stiffness within a centrifuge model can be accurately inferred by coupling measured surface deformation fields with a finite element model and applying a gradient-based optimization algorithm to minimize the error between observed and computed deformations.
proposals.method: The research will employ B. Kutter's expertise in centrifuge modeling and novel instrumentation. A series of centrifuge tests will be conducted on instrumented soil models with known, controlled spatial variations in density. High-resolution digital image correlation (DIC) will measure surface deformation fields under controlled loading. A finite element model (e.g., in OpenSees or PLAXIS) will simulate the test, and an inverse analysis framework will be implemented using a gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt) to iteratively adjust the spatial distribution of soil modulus and density in the numerical model until the computed surface deformations match the DIC measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, physics-informed methodology for non-destructively profiling soil properties from surface deformation data. It will advance the interpretation of centrifuge model tests by providing a quantitative link between global response and internal state. The resulting framework can improve the calibration of numerical models for predicting pile settlement and soil-structure interaction in spatially variable deposits.
proposals.novelty: While inverse analysis is known in geotechnics, its application to high-resolution deformation data from physical centrifuge models to resolve spatial property distributions is novel. This approach uniquely combines Kutter's advanced experimental techniques with numerical optimization to solve a classical geotechnical identification problem in a new, physically constrained context.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Investigation of Liquefaction-Induced Slope Deformation and Pile Downdrag in Heterogeneous, Medium-Dense Sand Deposits
proposals.motivation: Current centrifuge models and design guidelines for liquefaction-induced lateral spreading and pile downdrag often assume uniform, homogeneous soil deposits. In practice, natural deposits and compacted fills exhibit layering and density variations, which can significantly influence pore pressure dissipation, shear strain localization, and the resulting loads on embedded piles. Understanding these effects is critical for improving the predictive accuracy of numerical models and the safety of pile foundations in liquefiable slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: Intentional density variations and layering within a nominally medium-dense sand slope will (a) alter the pattern and magnitude of liquefaction-induced lateral deformation compared to a homogeneous deposit, and (b) impose spatially variable and potentially higher downdrag loads on pile foundations due to differential settlements and shear band formation at layer interfaces.
proposals.method: The research will employ centrifuge modeling at the University of California, Davis Center for Geotechnical Modeling. A novel rigid container with a transparent sidewall will be used. A sloping deposit will be prepared using Nevada sand, employing air pluviation to create controlled, interlayered zones of medium-dense (Dr ≈ 50-60%) and dense (Dr ≈ 70-80%) sand. Instrumentation will include pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement markers. A single instrumented aluminum pile will be installed in the slope. The model will be subjected to a series of base shaking events using a hydraulic shaker to trigger liquefaction. Deformation and load response will be measured via in-flight cameras and pile strain gauges.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic experimental dataset on the coupled soil-pile response in layered, medium-dense liquefiable slopes under controlled centrifuge conditions. It will deliver quantitative insights into how heterogeneity controls run-out distances and shear strain distribution. The results will inform the refinement of constitutive models and boundary conditions used in numerical simulations of non-homogeneous liquefiable soils.
proposals.novelty: While prior centrifuge studies by Kutter and others have examined liquefaction in uniform deposits, this proposal deliberately introduces controlled heterogeneity—a key field condition—into the experimental framework to study its isolated effects on a well-understood problem (pile downdrag in slopes), moving beyond the standard assumption of homogeneity.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Advanced Constitutive Models for Liquefaction-Induced Pile Downdrag and Lateral Spreading Using Standardized Centrifuge Data
proposals.motivation: Numerical modeling of liquefaction phenomena, such as pile downdrag and lateral spreading, is critical for performance-based design but suffers from a lack of high-quality, standardized experimental data for validation. Pre-2026 practice often relies on disparate case studies or idealized tests, leading to model calibration with limited predictive reliability for complex soil-structure interaction. This gap hinders the confident adoption of advanced constitutive models in engineering practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand, SANISAND) can be robustly calibrated and validated to accurately predict both the triggering of liquefaction and the subsequent soil deformation and pile load transfer observed in standardized centrifuge experiments. A consistent dataset will reveal the specific strengths and limitations of each model formulation.
proposals.method: The research will utilize the consistent, high-resolution datasets from prior centrifuge tests on uniform liquefiable deposits and fly ash run-out, conducted by the PI. These standardized experiments provide measured pore pressures, accelerations, soil displacements, and pile bending moments. The numerical validation will employ 2D/3D finite element analysis in platforms like OpenSees or FLAC, implementing and critically comparing state-of-the-art constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand for silica sand, a tailored model for fly ash). The calibration will follow a staged procedure, first matching element-level cyclic resistance, then validating against the full boundary value problem response.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available benchmark dataset and a documented protocol for calibrating liquefaction models against high-quality physical experiments. It will provide a clear evaluation of the predictive capability of existing constitutive models for coupled soil-pile response, directly informing future revisions of design guidelines. The results will strengthen the link between physical modeling, numerical simulation, and geotechnical practice.
proposals.novelty: While individual models have been tested, a systematic, side-by-side validation using the same meticulously instrumented and repeatable centrifuge experiments for both downdrag and lateral spreading scenarios is novel. The use of data from the PI's novel container, which minimizes boundary effects, provides an unprecedented standard for validation.

proposals.title: Quantifying Granular Kinematics in Liquefiable Soils: A Particle Tracking Velocimetry (PTV) Method for Centrifuge Modeling of Pile Downdrag
proposals.motivation: Current centrifuge modeling of liquefaction-induced pile downdrag relies on global measurements (e.g., pore pressure, pile load, settlement), which provide limited insight into the fundamental soil-structure interaction mechanisms at the grain scale. The kinematic behavior of the soil matrix during and after liquefaction, critical for predicting drag loads and settlements, remains poorly characterized. Adapting advanced optical measurement techniques from fluid dynamics to geotechnical centrifuge modeling can bridge this gap in understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Particle Tracking Velocimetry (PTV) to a transparent-sided centrifuge model will successfully resolve the displacement fields of individual sand grains during seismic liquefaction and subsequent reconsolidation. This will reveal that the spatial distribution of drag load on a pile is directly correlated with localized, non-uniform shear strains in the surrounding soil matrix.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a novel, rigid-walled centrifuge container with a transparent acrylic window, building on Kutter's prior work with instrumented containers. The soil model will be prepared with a thin, planar layer of dyed tracer particles amidst index-matched silica gel or a transparent soil surrogate to enable optical access. High-speed cameras mounted on the centrifuge package will capture images during shaking. Post-processing will utilize established PTV algorithms (e.g., OpenPIV or custom cross-correlation routines) to track particle displacements between frames, generating full-field velocity and strain maps in the soil surrounding an instrumented model pile.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first application of in-flight PTV to a geotechnical centrifuge, providing unprecedented, grain-scale kinematic data during liquefaction. It will validate and improve micromechanical explanations for macro-scale phenomena like downdrag. The resulting dataset will serve as a benchmark for calibrating advanced constitutive models and discrete element method (DEM) simulations of liquefaction.
proposals.novelty: While PTV is standard in fluid mechanics, its adaptation to a high-g centrifuge environment for tracking solid soil grains during dynamic loading is a significant, non-trivial innovation. This approach moves beyond post-mortem analysis (e.g., PIV on split containers) to capture real-time, in-situ kinematics, which has not been achieved in prior centrifuge studies of pile downdrag.

proposals.title: Direct Validation of Centrifuge Models: A Large-Scale 1g Shake Table Test of Fly Ash Slope Run-out and Pile Downdrag
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical earthquake engineering, but its scaling laws and boundary effects require validation against full-scale or large-scale prototype behavior. For complex, flow-type failures like the liquefaction-induced run-out of fly ash deposits and the resulting downdrag on piles, direct comparisons between centrifuge and 1g physical models are scarce. This gap limits confidence in extrapolating centrifuge results to field-scale design, particularly for critical infrastructure like pile foundations in industrial waste deposits.
proposals.hypothesis: The kinematic mechanisms, run-out distances, and pile downdrag loads observed in centrifuge tests of confined fly ash slopes will be replicated in a large-scale 1g shake table test when scaling laws are correctly applied, thereby validating the centrifuge modeling technique for this coupled soil-structure interaction problem.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a large, rigid soil container for a long-stroke, 1g shake table. The test geometry (slope angle, layer thicknesses, pile dimensions) will be a scaled-up, 1g equivalent of a previous centrifuge model, maintaining key dimensionless π-groups. We will instrument the model identically to centrifuge tests, using accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, displacement sensors (LVDTs/PIV), and strain gauges on model piles. The same fly ash material and preparation method will be used. The test will subject the model to a scaled input motion matching the centrifuge base shaking, capturing the complete run-out and pile response sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rare, direct validation dataset for centrifuge modeling of liquefaction-induced flow failures and associated pile downdrag. It will quantify potential scaling discrepancies and boundary effects, strengthening the theoretical foundation for using centrifuge tests in design. The results will offer clearer guidance for practicing engineers on translating centrifuge-derived design parameters to field conditions.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge and 1g tests are common in isolation, a direct, large-scale 1g replication of a specific centrifuge model for a coupled flow-slope-pile system is novel. This systematic comparison focuses explicitly on validating the centrifuge technique itself for a complex, post-liquefaction boundary value problem, rather than just studying the phenomenon.

proposals.title: Correlating Centrifuge Slope Run-out with Element-Level Cyclic Response: A Framework for Predicting Flow Failure in Geotechnical Wastes
proposals.motivation: The seismic stability of sloping deposits of industrial by-products like fly ash is a critical geotechnical challenge, with failure leading to catastrophic flow slides. While centrifuge modeling, as in Kutter's recent work, effectively captures the complex system-level run-out behavior, the results are project-specific and difficult to generalize. A predictive framework linking macro-scale slope performance to fundamental, measurable soil properties is needed for improved risk assessment and design.
proposals.hypothesis: The post-liquefaction run-out displacement of a model fly ash slope in a centrifuge test can be quantitatively predicted by the cyclic strength and strain accumulation characteristics of the same material measured in cyclic simple shear (CSS) element tests under comparable stress and density conditions.
proposals.method: The research will employ a paired experimental approach. First, identical fly ash samples will be prepared at target densities. A series of stress-controlled cyclic simple shear tests will be performed to establish the material's cyclic resistance ratio (CRR) and develop relationships between cyclic stress ratio (CSR), number of cycles to liquefaction, and post-cyclic shear strain. Concurrently, centrifuge model tests of slopes will be conducted using the novel container from prior work, instrumented to capture pore pressure generation and slope displacement during base shaking. The triggering and deformation response from the centrifuge will be directly compared to the CSS-derived soil parameters using a Newmark-type sliding block analysis modified to incorporate the strain-softening behavior quantified in the element tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, empirical correlation between element-level cyclic properties and system-level slope performance for geotechnical wastes, moving beyond qualitative observation. It will deliver a validated, mechanics-informed procedure for engineers to estimate run-out potential from standardized laboratory tests, enhancing the predictive capability for flow failure hazards.
proposals.novelty: While CSS and centrifuge tests are common, explicitly linking the post-liquefaction strain potential from CSS to the kinematic run-out in a centrifuge model for unstable slopes is novel. This bridges the gap between fundamental soil mechanics and physical system modeling for a critical failure mode.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Static Liquefaction and Run-out in Sloping Fly Ash Deposits: A Dual to Seismic Failure
proposals.motivation: The stability of industrial waste deposits, such as fly ash slopes, is a critical geotechnical concern. While past research, including Kutter's, has focused on seismic triggering, many failures occur under static or quasi-static loading conditions, such as during construction or due to rising pore pressures from infiltration. Understanding the mechanics of static liquefaction—where a loose, saturated slope collapses without dynamic shaking—is essential for improving hazard assessment and design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a critical combination of slope geometry, initial void ratio, and static shear stress can trigger liquefaction and significant flow failure in saturated, contractive fly ash slopes, and that the resulting run-out mechanisms and loads on embedded piles differ fundamentally from those induced by seismic loading.
proposals.method: The research will employ centrifuge modeling at UC Davis's Center for Geotechnical Modeling, using the novel instrumented container developed for fly ash run-out experiments. A series of models will be constructed with saturated, loose fly ash at a controlled slope. Static liquefaction will be triggered by in-flight increases in gravitational acceleration (centrifuge spin-up) or by localized loading at the slope crest. Advanced instrumentation, including pore pressure transducers, particle image velocimetry (PIV), and load cells on model piles, will capture the initiation, propagation, and consequences of the flow slide.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-quality, physical model data set on static liquefaction run-out in fly ash, directly complementing existing dynamic studies. It will yield new insights into the triggering conditions and post-liquefaction strength, informing stability analyses. The data on pile downdrag and lateral loading under static flow conditions will advance the design of pile foundations in similar geotechnical environments.
proposals.novelty: This study directly addresses the 'dual problem' to the researcher's prior seismic work, systematically investigating static triggering—a known but less experimentally studied hazard—using a proven, novel apparatus. It uniquely contrasts static and dynamic failure mechanisms for the same material within a single experimental framework.

proposals.title: Remote Characterization of Sand Consistency for Pile Design Using Site-Deployable Sensing and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Current geotechnical practice for characterizing sand consistency (e.g., relative density, SPT N-value) requires extracting and shipping physical samples to a central laboratory, a process that is time-consuming, costly, and can disturb the sample. For critical projects involving pile foundations in liquefiable deposits, this delay and uncertainty can impede design and increase risk. A remote characterization method would enable rapid, on-site assessment, particularly valuable for large or remote sites and for validating centrifuge model ground preparation.
proposals.hypothesis: That a combination of in-situ penetration resistance data and high-resolution site imagery, processed through a machine learning model, can accurately predict key sand consistency indices (e.g., relative density, friction angle) without the need for laboratory testing of shipped samples.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-stage approach. First, a database will be compiled from historical centrifuge tests and field case studies, pairing known soil properties with corresponding in-situ test data (e.g., miniature cone penetrometer traces) and overhead/down-borehole imagery. Second, a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture will be developed to fuse the time-series penetration data and visual texture features from images. The model will be trained to predict consistency indices, with validation performed using controlled centrifuge experiments where the target properties are known from pluviation methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for remote, non-destructive sand characterization, directly reducing the logistical burden of physical sample shipment. It will contribute a new, data-driven correlation tool for geotechnical engineers to estimate design parameters more efficiently. The method will also offer a way to digitally document and verify in-situ conditions for centrifuge model construction and back-analysis.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing exists, the direct fusion of in-situ mechanical penetration data with visual texture analysis using machine learning for geotechnical index prediction is novel. This approach moves beyond empirical correlations based solely on penetration resistance by incorporating spatially distributed visual data indicative of soil fabric.

proposals.title: Development of a Robotic System for Automated Density Measurement in LEAP Centrifuge Model Preparation
proposals.motivation: The Liquefaction Experiments and Analysis Projects (LEAP) rely on highly repeatable, uniform soil sample preparation to enable reliable validation of numerical models. The current manual procedure for measuring in-flight density using embedded sensors is operator-dependent, introducing a key source of variability that can obscure true soil-structure interaction mechanisms. Automating this critical step is essential to reduce experimental scatter and improve the fidelity of centrifuge data for validating constitutive models of liquefaction and pile downdrag.
proposals.hypothesis: A robotic system, using precision actuators and computer vision for sensor placement, can perform the LEAP density measurement procedure with significantly lower variability than manual methods, as quantified by the coefficient of variation in repeated density measurements on identical samples.
proposals.method: The research will design and build a semi-automated gantry system for a standard centrifuge strongbox. The system will integrate a servo-controlled vertical actuator to lower a conductivity-type density probe into pre-defined grid locations. A machine vision subsystem, using a calibrated overhead camera and fiducial markers on the container, will automate probe positioning and depth logging. Control software will be developed in Python (using OpenCV for vision and a Raspberry Pi/Arduino stack for motion control) to execute the standardized LEAP measurement sequence, logging time-stamped depth and conductivity data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a prototype tool and an open-source control framework for automated geotechnical sample characterization, directly increasing the reproducibility of LEAP and similar centrifuge experiments. It will provide a quantitative benchmark for operator-induced variability in physical modeling, leading to more reliable calibration data for numerical models of liquefaction-induced settlement and pile downdrag.
proposals.novelty: While automation exists for laboratory index testing, applying robotic precision to the specific, delicate task of in-situ density measurement within a centrifuge model container during preparation is novel. The integration of computer vision for spatial registration directly addresses the alignment challenges unique to instrumented strongboxes.

proposals.title: Quantifying Sand Density and Flow Potential via Pharmaceutical Powder Characterization Techniques: A Centrifuge Validation Study
proposals.motivation: In geotechnical engineering, the initial density and packing state of sand are critical for predicting its mechanical response, including liquefaction potential and settlement. Current in-situ and laboratory methods for density characterization can be indirect or lack standardization for capturing flow-related properties. The pharmaceutical industry has developed rigorous, standardized tests (e.g., Carr Index, Hausner Ratio, angle of repose, shear cell testing) to quantify powder flowability and packing, which are analogous to granular soil behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: Standardized pharmaceutical powder flowability indices (Carr Index, Hausner Ratio) and packing tests can provide a rapid, reproducible, and quantitative measure of sand density state and its susceptibility to flow-type deformations, correlating directly with geotechnical centrifuge test outcomes for phenomena like liquefaction-induced settlement and run-out.
proposals.method: We will apply ASTM/Ph.Eur. standard powder tests to several uniform sands (e.g., Ottawa F-65) prepared at a range of relative densities. Tests will include tapped density analyzers for Hausner Ratio, rotational shear cells for flow function coefficients, and automated angle of repose measurement. The resulting indices will be correlated with the sand's behavior in a series of centrifuge model tests. These will replicate key scenarios from the researcher's prior work, such as pile downdrag in liquefiable deposits and the run-out of slopes following a loss of confinement, using the novel instrumented container. The centrifuge results (settlement, pore pressure, displacement) will serve as ground truth to validate the powder-derived metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, standardized laboratory protocol for rapidly assessing sand density and flow potential, providing a direct link between small-scale powder physics and large-scale geotechnical performance. It will deliver quantitative correlations between pharmaceutical indices and critical state soil mechanics parameters, offering practitioners a novel tool for initial site characterization and material screening.
proposals.novelty: While powder testing is standard in pharmaceuticals, its systematic application to natural sands for predicting geotechnical centrifuge-scale behavior is unexplored. The novelty lies in cross-pollinating these highly developed industrial standards with geotechnical physical modeling to create a new characterization framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying Granular Consistency in Geotechnical Centrifuge Models: An Automated Image Analysis Framework for Fly Ash and Silica Sands
proposals.motivation: In centrifuge modeling of soil-structure interaction and flow failures, the consistency of granular material properties (e.g., grain size distribution, shape) between tests is critical for repeatability and reliable scaling. Current sieve and manual microscopy methods are time-consuming and provide limited shape data. A rapid, quantitative method is needed to ensure specimen consistency, particularly for materials like fly ash used in recent run-out and pile downdrag studies, where initial state dictates liquefaction and deformation behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: Automated digital image analysis (DIA) can reliably and rapidly quantify the grain size distribution and shape descriptors (e.g., sphericity, roundness) of fly ash and standard silica sands used in centrifuge modeling, providing a more consistent and informative material characterization than traditional sieve analysis alone.
proposals.method: High-resolution digital micrographs of dispersed fly ash and silica sand samples will be acquired using a standardized optical microscope setup. Images will be processed using established pre-2026 DIA algorithms (e.g., in ImageJ/FIJI with watershed segmentation) to isolate individual grains. Key metrics will be extracted: equivalent diameter (for size distribution), and shape factors (circularity, aspect ratio). The method's precision will be validated against laser diffraction and manual sieve analysis. The protocol will be applied to batches of material before and after centrifuge tests to assess degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a standardized, efficient protocol for granular material quality control in geotechnical centrifuge labs. It will provide novel data linking grain shape to the mechanical behavior observed in prior centrifuge tests (e.g., fly ash run-out, pile downdrag). The outcome will be a practical tool to reduce experimental scatter and improve the interpretation of model tests involving particulate materials.
proposals.novelty: While DIA is common in geology, its systematic application for routine quality assurance and state correlation in dynamic centrifuge modeling, especially for industrial by-products like fly ash, is novel. It moves beyond basic gradation to incorporate quantitative shape metrics, which are rarely measured but critically influence liquefaction and shear strength.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Continuous In-Situ Density Monitoring System for Centrifuge Model Preparation of Granular Soils
proposals.motivation: In geotechnical centrifuge modeling, the initial density of sand deposits is a critical state variable controlling soil strength, stiffness, and liquefaction susceptibility.
