Current preparation methods, such as air pluviation, rely on indirect calibration (e.g., drop height, mass) and discrete, post-placement verification (e.g., tube samples), which are labor-intensive and provide only point measurements. This introduces significant uncertainty in model repeatability and the precise initial conditions for experiments on pile settlement, soil-structure interaction, and flow slides.
proposals.hypothesis: A system combining non-invasive, continuous sensing with real-time data processing can accurately map the evolving density field during sand pluviation in a centrifuge container, enabling direct control and verification of model homogeneity.
proposals.method: We will develop a system using an array of low-frequency piezoelectric bender elements (pre-2026 standard for shear wave velocity, Vs, measurement) embedded at multiple depths and lateral positions in a novel, instrumented centrifuge container wall. Shear wave velocity is related to small-strain shear modulus (Gmax), which is a strong function of void ratio and effective stress. During pluviation, automated, continuous triggering of the bender elements will generate Vs profiles. A real-time algorithm, implementing the established relationship Gmax ~ (e)^-a * (σ')^b (where e is void ratio and σ' is effective stress), will invert the Vs data to estimate relative density, accounting for the increasing overburden stress as deposition proceeds. The system will be calibrated and validated against traditional density measurements in controlled pluviation tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first methodology for real-time, spatially distributed density monitoring during centrifuge model construction. It will directly improve the accuracy and repeatability of physical models for liquefaction, pile downdrag, and slope stability studies. The resulting dataset will also offer unprecedented insight into the spatial variability inherent in pluviation techniques.
proposals.novelty: While bender elements are used for post-placement soil characterization, their application as a continuous, in-process monitoring tool during model preparation is novel. The integration of real-time inverse analysis to output a continuous density field, rather than discrete Vs values, represents a significant advancement over existing quality control methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Characterization of Sand Provenance: A Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Source and Handling History from Index Properties
proposals.motivation: In geotechnical engineering, the mechanical behavior of sands is highly sensitive to their source and handling history, which can alter index properties like gradation and density in subtle ways. Current practice treats these properties as inputs for design but does not use them to trace material origin, a critical gap for forensic analysis of foundation performance or for quality control in large projects using sand from multiple suppliers. This inverse problem bridges soil characterization and material traceability.
proposals.hypothesis: The combined statistical signature of standard index properties (grain size distribution, specific gravity, minimum and maximum void ratios) contains sufficient information to classify a sand sample's geological source and distinguish between different handling or placement histories (e.g., pluviated vs. tamped) with significant accuracy.
proposals.method: The research will employ a supervised machine learning approach. A curated database will be compiled from published literature and in-house centrifuge test records (leveraging Kutter's expertise), tagging each sand entry with known source and preparation method. Feature vectors will include full gradation curves (via curve-fitting parameters), index densities, and derived uniformity metrics. Classification algorithms, including Random Forests and Support Vector Machines, will be trained and validated to predict source and handling class. Dimensionality reduction (PCA) will be used to visualize the feature space and identify the most discriminatory properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel, data-driven framework for the forensic sourcing of sands, providing a practical tool for verifying material consistency in construction and investigating the root causes of anomalous soil behavior. It will establish quantitative links between processing history and measurable index properties, enhancing the interpretation of routine site investigation data.
proposals.novelty: While index properties are used to predict forward engineering behavior, using them to solve the inverse problem of deducing source and history is a fundamentally new application. The integration of geotechnical database curation with machine learning classification for provenance tracking is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Micro-mechanical Interrogation of Soil-Footing Interfaces to Validate the Constant Rotation Assumption for Extreme Geomaterials
proposals.motivation: The constant rotation assumption, which simplifies the failure mechanism beneath shallow foundations, is a cornerstone of macro-scale bearing capacity models. Its validity for extreme soils like crushable pumice or cemented sands, where particle-scale behavior dominates, remains untested, posing a risk to the design of infrastructure on marginal or hazardous sites. This gap limits the physical basis of foundation design, particularly for scenarios involving seismic loading or unusual geomaterials where interface mechanics are critical.
proposals.hypothesis: The constant rotation assumption is not a universal emergent property but breaks down for extreme geomaterials due to distinct micro-mechanical interface phenomena, such as particle crushing, cementation bond rupture, or fabric reorientation, which govern the initiation of failure and moment mobilization.
proposals.method: We will perform systematic nano-indentation and micro-scratching experiments using a Hysitron TI Premier or equivalent system on prepared specimens of extreme soils (e.g., crushable pumice, artificially cemented silica sand, and sensitive clay). The soil surfaces will be indented and scratched by diamond tips with geometries (flat, conical, rounded) mimicking idealized footing shapes. We will measure the load-displacement response, hardness, elastic modulus, and fracture energy at the interface to characterize the initiation of failure. Data will be analyzed to extract the mobilized moment and rotation characteristics at the micro-scale, directly comparing them to the macro-scale assumption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, particle-scale evidence for or against the constant rotation assumption, linking micro-mechanical interface properties to macro-scale foundation models. It will yield a new dataset on the fundamental interface mechanics of extreme soils, informing more physically accurate design guidelines for foundations on challenging ground.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of advanced nano-mechanical testing, a tool from materials science, with a core geotechnical engineering paradigm to directly probe its micro-foundations is novel. Applying this to extreme soils, beyond typical sands and clays, to challenge a long-standing macro-scale assumption represents a significant departure from conventional validation methods.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Inherent Soil Heterogeneity on Pile Downdrag and Settlement via Seismic Tomography in Centrifuge Models
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge modeling of pile foundations in liquefiable soils often assumes a uniform, homogeneous soil profile for both test setup and numerical validation. However, inherent spatial variability in density and saturation, even in carefully prepared models, introduces unquantified uncertainty. This discrepancy can lead to misinterpretation of pile load-settlement behavior and downdrag forces, limiting the reliability of physical models for design validation.
proposals.hypothesis: That seismic wave velocity and attenuation tomography can map the inherent spatial heterogeneity of soil density and saturation within a centrifuge model container, and that incorporating this empirical heterogeneity map into numerical simulations will significantly improve the prediction of measured pile settlement and downdrag loads compared to a uniform soil assumption.
proposals.method: A novel centrifuge container, instrumented with an embedded perimeter array of piezoelectric bender elements (sources) and accelerometers (receivers), will be developed. Prior to and during centrifuge spin-up, shear wave velocity (Vs) and attenuation profiles will be generated using established travel-time and amplitude inversion tomography algorithms (e.g., Simultaneous Iterative Reconstruction Technique). This non-destructive imaging will be applied to a fly ash or silica sand model containing an instrumented pile, replicating downdrag test conditions. The reconstructed 3D Vs field, correlated to relative density, will serve as direct input for a 3D finite element model in OpenSees or PLAXIS to simulate the experiment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, high-resolution maps of actual soil heterogeneity in a geotechnical centrifuge model, creating a benchmark for model setup quality. It will directly quantify how inherent variability affects key pile performance metrics, advancing the interpretation of physical modeling data. The integrated imaging-and-modeling framework will set a new standard for validating numerical simulations against physical tests.
proposals.novelty: While geophysical methods are used in field site characterization, their adaptation as an internal, in-situ diagnostic tool for mapping heterogeneity within a laboratory-scale physical model is novel. This bridges the gap between assumed homogeneity in physical models and recognized heterogeneity in the field, using medical imaging principles directly in a geotechnical engineering context.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Pile Downdrag and Settlement in Layered and Variable Soil Profiles
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge modeling of pile-soil interaction, particularly for liquefaction-induced downdrag and settlement, has predominantly relied on uniform soil deposits for experimental control. However, natural and engineered soil deposits are often layered or spatially variable, which can significantly alter stress redistribution, drainage conditions, and failure mechanisms. This gap between idealized laboratory models and realistic field conditions limits the direct applicability of many experimental findings to design practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing layered soil profiles—specifically, interbedded layers of liquefiable sand and low-permeability silt or clay—will fundamentally alter the development of excess pore pressures, the timing and magnitude of downdrag loads on piles, and the post-liquefaction settlement patterns compared to behavior in uniform liquefiable deposits.
proposals.method: The research will employ a series of centrifuge tests at the 100g-tonne centrifuge facility. A novel, instrumented container will be used to prepare controlled, layered soil models combining Nevada sand and a silt-like material (e.g., silica flour or fly ash). Instrumentation will include pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, LVDTs, and strain gauges on model piles. The experimental sequence will involve: 1) Characterizing the baseline response of a uniform liquefiable deposit, 2) Testing profiles with a sealing cap layer, and 3) Testing interbedded layered systems. All models will be subjected to a consistent base shaking event to trigger liquefaction, with data focused on pore pressure dissipation, pile strain, and soil surface settlement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic centrifuge dataset on pile downdrag in layered, partially-draining soil systems, quantifying the effects of layering on drag load magnitude and distribution. It will deliver practical insights for refining analytical and numerical models used in practice, moving them beyond the assumption of soil homogeneity. The findings will directly inform the design of pile foundations in stratified deposits prone to liquefaction.
proposals.novelty: While prior centrifuge studies by Kutter and others have advanced understanding in uniform soils, this proposal deliberately challenges that foundational simplification. The novelty lies in the controlled, physical modeling of the coupled hydromechanical effects arising from soil heterogeneity, a critical step towards more realistic physical simulation of in-situ conditions.

proposals.title: Development of Semi-Empirical Models for Foundation Settlement and Rotation Using a Compiled Centrifuge Database (FoRCy)
proposals.motivation: Predicting foundation settlement and rotation under complex loading remains a critical challenge in geotechnical design, often relying on simplified analytical methods or site-specific numerical modeling. The FoRCy database, compiled from numerous centrifuge tests, provides a unique, high-quality dataset of soil-structure interaction phenomena under controlled, repeatable conditions. Leveraging this pre-2026 resource can bridge the gap between idealized theory and the complex reality captured in physical models, leading to more reliable design tools.
proposals.hypothesis: That systematic analysis of the FoRCy database will reveal consistent, quantifiable relationships between soil properties, pile characteristics, loading conditions, and the resulting foundation settlement and rotation, enabling the development of robust predictive models.
proposals.method: The research will first perform a comprehensive statistical analysis and feature engineering on the FoRCy database to identify key dimensionless parameters influencing settlement and rotation. Subsequently, we will develop and compare multiple model forms, including multivariate regression and machine learning algorithms (specifically, Random Forest and Gradient Boosting models, given their pre-2026 prevalence for handling complex, non-linear geotechnical data). Model training will use a subset of the database, with validation and performance assessment conducted on a held-out test set and against independent centrifuge experiments not included in FoRCy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver new, database-calibrated semi-empirical equations and/or validated machine learning models for predicting foundation displacement. It will provide practicing engineers with more accurate and accessible tools for serviceability limit state design. Furthermore, the analysis will highlight critical data gaps to guide future centrifuge testing campaigns.
proposals.novelty: While databases exist for field case histories, this proposal uniquely utilizes a large, high-fidelity *centrifuge* database (FoRCy) for model development, allowing isolation and systematic study of fundamental mechanisms without the confounding variability of in-situ conditions. The integration of classical geotechnical parameters with modern data science techniques on this curated dataset is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Dimensional Analysis of Pile-Soil Moment Interaction: Defining the Applicability Limits of a Constant Rotation Criterion
proposals.motivation: In geotechnical practice, a pile rotation of approximately 0.01 radians is often empirically used as a criterion for mobilizing the ultimate moment capacity. However, the universality of this value is questionable, as it likely depends on soil properties, pile geometry, and loading conditions. A rigorous, physics-based framework is needed to define the limits of this rule-of-thumb, ensuring safe and economical pile design for lateral loading scenarios like seismic events or wind.
proposals.hypothesis: The 0.01 radian rotation criterion corresponds to a specific, constant value of a key dimensionless group derived from the governing parameters of pile-soil moment interaction. This criterion is only valid within a bounded region of the full dimensionless parameter space.
proposals.method: The Buckingham π theorem will be applied to the pile-soil-moment system, defining dimensionless groups (π-terms) from variables including rotation (θ), soil modulus (E_s), undrained shear strength (s_u), pile diameter (D), embedment length (L), bending stiffness (EI), and load eccentricity (e). A comprehensive parametric study will be conducted using 3D finite element analysis (e.g., in PLAXIS 3D or Abaqus) with validated soil constitutive models. Key tests will be physically validated using centrifuge modeling in a newly instrumented container, employing techniques from the researcher's prior work on instrumented containers and pile behavior in liquefiable deposits.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated dimensionless framework that explicitly maps the conditions under which the constant rotation criterion is applicable. It will provide practicing engineers with a scientifically grounded method to assess when the 0.01 radian rule is conservative, non-conservative, or appropriate, moving design beyond empirical reliance.
proposals.novelty: While dimensional analysis is standard in fluid mechanics, its rigorous application to define the limits of a specific geotechnical deformation criterion (0.01 rad rotation) is novel. This approach systematically bridges empirical observation and theoretical similitude for a fundamental pile design problem.

proposals.title: Validating Centrifuge Scaling Laws for Pile Downdrag and Foundation Settlement via Instrumented Field Pilots
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of foundation design, but its predictive reliability for full-scale performance, particularly for complex soil-structure interaction phenomena like liquefaction-induced downdrag, relies on assumed scaling laws. A direct, quantitative validation of these scaling laws using controlled, full-scale field data is rarely performed, creating an uncertainty gap between physical models and real-world foundation behavior. This research directly addresses this validation gap, enhancing confidence in using centrifuge databases for critical infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: That rigorously derived scaling laws from dimensional analysis, when applied to a comprehensive centrifuge model database, can accurately predict the load-settlement and downdrag response of full-scale pile foundations in controlled, well-characterized field conditions, with any discrepancies directly quantifying scale effects.
proposals.method: The research will first perform a dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem) for pile settlement and downdrag, identifying key dimensionless groups (e.g., normalized settlement, stress level, density index). Existing centrifuge data from the researcher's prior work on uniform liquefiable deposits and novel containers will be synthesized into a predictive model based on these groups. Subsequently, a series of fully instrumented (with strain gauges, piezometers, inclinometers) test piles will be installed in a well-characterized, uniform soil site (e.g., a calibrated sand deposit). These field pilots will be subjected to staged loading and, if feasible, induced liquefaction, while monitoring their response. The core of the method is the direct, quantitative comparison of measured field performance against predictions made by applying the scaling laws to the centrifuge model data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rare, direct validation dataset linking centrifuge model performance to full-scale field behavior, quantitatively confirming or refining existing scaling laws for foundation settlement and downdrag. It will produce a documented methodology for field validation of physical models and pinpoint specific scale-dependent mechanisms (e.g., soil fabric effects, installation processes) that may not be fully replicated in centrifuge tests.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge modeling and field testing are common, the systematic application of dimensional analysis to create a predictive model from an existing centrifuge database, followed by its direct validation against a purpose-built, instrumented field pilot under controlled conditions, represents a novel, aerospace-inspired validation paradigm in geotechnical engineering.

proposals.title: Bayesian Fusion of Centrifuge-Modeled Pile Rocking and In-Situ Data for Constrained Soil Parameter Estimation
proposals.motivation: Estimating soil properties for foundation design often relies on sparse, localized site investigation data, leading to significant uncertainty in predicting pile settlement and rocking response under load. This uncertainty is particularly critical for structures on liquefiable or spatially variable deposits, where conventional back-analysis from pile response alone is an ill-posed inverse problem. A robust framework is needed to integrate direct site characterization with observed foundation performance to reduce geotechnical parameter uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian updating framework, which treats the rocking response of a pile as a likelihood constraint on a prior soil model derived from direct site investigation, will yield a posterior distribution of soil strength and stiffness parameters with significantly reduced uncertainty compared to using either data source independently.
proposals.method: The research will employ centrifuge modeling to generate the 'field' rocking response data. A novel instrumented container will be used to create layered soil profiles, including liquefiable fly ash deposits, instrumented with piles. Direct investigation data (e.g., CPT, Vs) will be simulated via in-flight actuators and sensors. A Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm will be implemented to perform the Bayesian updating, where the forward model is a validated numerical simulation of soil-pile interaction. The likelihood function will quantify the misfit between simulated and measured rocking responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated probabilistic methodology for fusing geophysical/penetration test data with foundation performance observations, directly addressing the inverse problem's ill-posedness. It will deliver practical guidance on how rocking response, often considered a performance metric, can be quantitatively used to refine subsurface models for more reliable pile design.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods exist in geotechnics, the explicit use of centrifuge-modeled foundation rocking as a likelihood constraint to update a prior from simulated in-situ tests is novel. This approach moves beyond deterministic back-analysis or purely statistical methods by physically linking the two data sources within a controlled, repeatable experimental framework.

proposals.title: A Proficiency Testing Framework for Miniature Cone Penetration Test Calibration: An Inter-Laboratory Comparison Protocol Adapted from Clinical Chemistry
proposals.motivation: Miniature CPTs are critical for interpreting centrifuge model tests of pile settlement and soil liquefaction, but their calibration and data quality are laboratory-dependent, hindering direct comparison of results. The lack of standardized verification protocols undermines confidence in shared datasets and the reliability of derived design parameters. This inconsistency poses a significant barrier to collaborative research and the validation of numerical models against physical experiments.
proposals.hypothesis: That a formal inter-laboratory comparison program, modeled on clinical chemistry proficiency testing, can systematically identify and quantify systematic errors in miniature CPT measurements, leading to improved calibration practices and data harmonization across geotechnical centrifuge facilities.
proposals.method: The research will design and execute a pilot ILC program. A central team will fabricate homogeneous, stable reference soil specimens (e.g., uniform silica sand, lightly cemented silt) with thoroughly characterized engineering properties using conventional lab tests. These specimens, along with a strict testing protocol covering setup, calibration, and data acquisition, will be distributed to participating centrifuge labs. Each lab will perform miniature CPT tests and submit raw tip resistance and sleeve friction data. Centralized analysis will employ robust statistical methods, including z-score calculation (z = (lab result - assigned value) / standard deviation for proficiency assessment) to benchmark each participant against the reference values and the peer group distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first standardized framework for verifying miniature CPT performance, providing labs with quantitative metrics for their calibration quality. It will produce a publicly available database of inter-laboratory variability for key soil parameters, setting a new benchmark for data quality in physical modeling. The protocol will serve as a model for standardizing other shared sensor systems in geotechnical experimentation.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in geotechnical engineering as it directly adapts a mature quality assurance paradigm from an unrelated field (clinical laboratory science) to solve a persistent instrumentation challenge. It moves beyond ad-hoc calibration checks to a formal, statistical, and recurring system of performance verification.

proposals.title: A Calibration-Enabled Inverse Framework for Global Soil Property Assessment Using Standardized Miniature CPT Rocking Response
proposals.motivation: In-situ soil characterization via Cone Penetration Test (CPT) is fundamental to geotechnical design, but results are inherently influenced by device-specific characteristics, particularly the dynamic rocking response during penetration. This introduces significant uncertainty when comparing data from different sites or labs, undermining the reliability of inverse analyses for determining soil strength and stiffness. A standardized, device-agnostic framework is needed to isolate true soil behavior from measurement artifacts, especially for centrifuge modeling where miniature CPT devices vary widely.
proposals.hypothesis: That a rigorous, lab-based calibration protocol can quantify the unique dynamic rocking 'fingerprint' of any miniature CPT device, and that using this fingerprint to pre-process field data will enable a universally accurate inverse solution for soil properties, independent of the specific hardware used.
proposals.method: The research will first develop and validate a standardized centrifuge-based calibration procedure using a novel instrumented container to characterize the dynamic rocking response of multiple miniature CPT devices across different labs. Calibrated device transfer functions will be derived. These functions will then be integrated into an inverse analysis framework, likely employing a Bayesian updating algorithm or an optimization routine (e.g., a genetic algorithm), to pre-process a global database of CPT soundings. The inversion will back-calculate fundamental soil parameters (e.g., friction angle, shear modulus) by iteratively matching simulated responses to the normalized, device-corrected field measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first universally applicable protocol for calibrating miniature CPT devices, directly addressing a key source of error in physical modeling. It will deliver a robust, open-source inverse analysis tool that yields more reliable and comparable soil property estimates from CPT data worldwide, enhancing the safety and economy of foundation design, particularly for pile settlement and downdrag assessments in variable deposits.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of rigorous device calibration with global-scale inverse analysis is novel; previous work has either focused on calibration in isolation or on inverse methods without systematically accounting for hardware-specific dynamic artifacts. This approach creates a 'virtual standard penetrometer' to normalize all data prior to inversion.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Validation of a Novel Cone Penetrometer Device in Stratified and Sloping Soil Profiles with Embedded Piles
proposals.motivation: In-situ testing via cone penetration test (CPT) is critical for characterizing soil stratigraphy and parameters for geotechnical design. While centrifuge CPT devices are powerful tools for model-scale site investigation, their validation has been largely confined to uniform deposits, which poorly represent real-world conditions involving layers, slopes, and existing foundations. Extending validation to these complex geometries is essential for reliable application of centrifuge CPT data to prototype-scale engineering problems, such as assessing pile downdrag in layered liquefiable soils or slope stability.
proposals.hypothesis: The novel centrifuge CPT device, previously tested in uniform fly ash and sand deposits, will provide accurate and repeatable measurements of tip resistance and sleeve friction in non-uniform soil profiles, and its data will correlate effectively with known model stratigraphy and the measured response of embedded pile foundations.
proposals.method: The research will employ the existing, instrumented centrifuge CPT device within a geotechnical centrifuge. A series of models will be constructed in a rigid container, featuring: 1) layered profiles of saturated silica sand and clayey silt, 2) a sloping deposit of loose, saturated sand, and 3) models with pre-installed instrumented model piles. The CPT will be performed at multiple locations in each model at 50-g. Data will be compared against known model construction records, pore pressure transducer readings, and pile load-settlement responses. The analysis will focus on the CPT's ability to detect layer boundaries, slope-induced stress variations, and the presence of stiff inclusions (piles).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic validation of a centrifuge CPT device in complex, realistic soil models, directly building on Kutter's prior work on fly ash run-out and pile downdrag. It will produce a robust protocol for using centrifuge CPT in stratified site investigation, enhancing the fidelity of physical modeling for problems involving soil-structure interaction and spatially variable ground conditions.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge CPT is established, its rigorous validation has not moved beyond simple, uniform deposits. This proposal uniquely integrates the device into experimental geometries directly relevant to ongoing research themes (pile downdrag, slope instability) to test its performance limits and practical utility in a controlled but complex environment.

proposals.title: Development of a Micro-Penetrometer for Grain-Scale Investigation of Pile-Soil Interaction in Analog Centrifuge Models
proposals.motivation: The interpretation of centrifuge model tests on pile foundations in granular soils relies on continuum-scale metrics like cone penetration resistance (qc). However, the fundamental mechanisms governing pile load transfer and downdrag are controlled by grain-scale interactions and force chain networks. A tool to directly measure these micro-mechanical responses within physical models is lacking, creating a disconnect between observed macro-behavior and its particulate origins.
proposals.hypothesis: A miniaturized, instrumented micro-penetrometer (diameter < 1 mm) can resolve the spatially heterogeneous, grain-scale mechanical response within an analog granular deposit, and these measurements will correlate with and help explain macro-scale phenomena like pile settlement and liquefaction-induced downdrag observed in centrifuge tests.
proposals.method: The project will design, fabricate, and calibrate a micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS)-based penetrometer with a tip diameter of 500-800 µm, instrumented with a micro-load cell and pore pressure transducer. It will be deployed via a high-precision micro-actuator within transparent soil analogs (e.g., fused quartz or refractive-index-matched materials) in a benchtop setup. Penetration tests will be performed in deposits with controlled density and stress history, prepared in a novel small-scale container. Force-displacement data will be synchronized with particle image velocimetry (PIV) to link local resistance to grain kinematics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, grain-scale mechanical probe for physical models, creating a new dataset linking micro-mechanics to macro-response. It will advance fundamental granular physics by quantifying the scale effect between particle- and continuum-scale strength. For geotechnical practice, it will offer a new validation tool for discrete element method (DEM) simulations of pile-soil interaction.
proposals.novelty: While micro-penetration exists in bio-tissues and powders, its application as a diagnostic tool within geotechnical centrifuge analog models is novel. The integration of a MEMS-based micro-CPT with transparent soil and PIV for simultaneous measurement of force chains and penetration resistance represents a significant methodological advance beyond existing macro-scale centrifuge instrumentation.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Pile-Soil Interaction in Warming Permafrost: Development of a Modified Cone Penetration Test for Frozen Soils
proposals.motivation: Infrastructure stability in warming permafrost regions is a critical geotechnical challenge. Current design methods for piles in frozen ground rely on empirical correlations from field tests, which are costly and difficult to scale for physical modeling. There is a pre-2026 gap in validated centrifuge-scale testing techniques to simulate the high-strength, temperature-sensitive behavior of frozen soils and the subsequent loss of strength upon thaw.
proposals.hypothesis: A miniaturized cone penetrometer (CPT) system, modified with temperature control and a high-capacity load cell, can successfully characterize the scaled strength profile of frozen soil models in a centrifuge, enabling the direct study of pile downdrag and settlement during simulated thaw.
proposals.method: The research will adapt the existing centrifuge CPT apparatus used by Kutter for liquefiable soils. A temperature-controlled model container, inspired by prior fly ash work, will be developed to create and maintain a frozen soil profile. The CPT tip will be instrumented with a miniature thermocouple and a high-capacity load cell to measure tip resistance and sleeve friction at low temperatures under high g. Scaling laws for ice-soil mixture strength and thermal diffusion will be applied. A series of centrifuge tests will first calibrate the CPT response in frozen sand-ice mixtures at various temperatures, followed by tests on instrumented model piles to measure thaw-induced downdrag loads.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel centrifuge modeling methodology for frozen ground, delivering a validated scaling framework for CPT data in frozen soils. It will provide first-of-their-kind physical model data on pile load transfer in thawing permafrost, directly informing the calibration of numerical models and improving predictive capacity for infrastructure risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge CPT is standard for soft soils, its application to characterize the high strength and temperature-dependent behavior of frozen soils is novel. The integration of in-situ thermal control with penetration testing in a geotechnical centrifuge to model thaw processes represents a significant methodological advance beyond static frozen strength tests.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Analysis of Pile Downdrag in Layered Liquefiable Soils: A Targeted Re-examination of Centrifuge Test Data
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction-induced downdrag remains a critical design challenge for deep foundations in seismic regions. While prior centrifuge studies have established the phenomenon in uniform deposits, real soil profiles are often layered, combining liquefiable and non-liquefiable strata. A detailed, mechanistic understanding of how soil stratigraphy and post-liquefaction reconsolidation interact to govern the magnitude and distribution of downdrag loads on piles is needed to refine analytical models and design codes.
proposals.hypothesis: The magnitude and temporal evolution of downdrag load on a pile in a layered liquefiable deposit are governed by the relative thickness and permeability of the liquefiable layer and the stiffness of the underlying competent layer, leading to a non-uniform load distribution not predicted by current simplified design methods.
proposals.method: This study will perform a focused re-analysis of existing centrifuge test data from the 'Closure to...' paper and related experiments. Specific test subsets will be selected based on stratigraphy (e.g., uniform sand vs. sand over clay) and pile tip conditions (floating vs. end-bearing). High-resolution time-series data for pore pressure, pile bending moment, and axial load will be analyzed using signal processing to isolate phases of liquefaction triggering, drag load development, and dissipation. Dimensional analysis and back-calculation of soil-pile interface shear stresses will be performed to develop a stratigraphy-informed framework for estimating downdrag.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, mechanics-based interpretation of downdrag in layered systems, directly informing the next generation of design guidelines (e.g., updates to API, AASHTO, or Eurocode provisions). It will also deliver a curated dataset and analysis methodology for calibrating advanced numerical models that simulate soil-pile interaction during and after liquefaction.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous studies focusing on global response in uniform deposits, this analysis targets the specific, under-explored mechanics of downdrag in stratified profiles by leveraging high-fidelity, existing centrifuge data through a targeted, phase-based analytical framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Influence of Relative Density and Shaking Intensity on Pile Downdrag and Soil Run-out: A Data-Driven Model from Centrifuge Experiments
proposals.motivation: Current design for piles in liquefiable soils relies on simplified correlations between shaking intensity (PGA) and soil response (e.g., lateral displacement, downdrag), often treating soil density as a secondary factor. This can lead to overly conservative or unconservative designs, especially for materials like fly ash with complex post-liquefaction behavior. A refined, multi-variable predictive model is needed to improve the reliability of foundation performance assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The relationship between shaking intensity and soil response (pile downdrag force, lateral run-out displacement) is not linear but is strongly modulated by the initial relative density of the soil deposit, and this interaction can be captured through a robust statistical model derived from centrifuge data.
proposals.method: We will compile a unified dataset from prior centrifuge tests on uniform sands and fly ash, including key parameters: relative density (Dr), peak ground acceleration (PGA), excess pore pressure ratio (ru), pile downdrag load, and soil run-out distance. Using pre-2026 machine learning techniques, we will develop and compare multiple regression models, including multivariate polynomial regression and a Random Forest regressor, to identify the dominant features and their interactions. The model will be trained and validated using a k-fold cross-validation approach to ensure generalizability beyond the specific test conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a calibrated, quantitative framework for predicting key soil-structure interaction phenomena, directly informing the next generation of performance-based design guidelines for foundations in liquefiable ground. It will also demonstrate the value of synthesizing disparate centrifuge datasets to extract broader geotechnical principles.
proposals.novelty: While individual centrifuge studies exist, this research uniquely synthesizes data from tests on both downdrag and run-out to develop a unified predictive model that explicitly quantifies the coupled effect of density and shaking intensity, moving beyond single-variable correlations.

proposals.title: Systematic Quantification of Inter-Facility Variability in Centrifuge Modeling of Pile Downdrag in Liquefiable Soils
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical physical modeling, but significant differences in results for nominally identical tests conducted at different facilities (e.g., LEAP, VELACS) undermine confidence in the method's repeatability and the universality of derived design principles. This variability, observed in studies of pile downdrag and soil liquefaction, creates uncertainty in calibrating numerical models and transferring findings to practice. A systematic investigation is needed to move from anecdotal observation to a quantified understanding of the primary error sources.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary sources of inter-facility result divergence in centrifuge pile tests are not random but can be attributed to a hierarchy of factors, with subtle differences in boundary conditions (container flexibility, membrane effects), soil preparation and saturation procedures, and actuator control dynamics being more influential than minor variations in pile properties or instrumentation.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coordinated, multi-facility test program using a standardized pile-downdrag in liquefiable sand experiment. A detailed pre-test characterization protocol will be enforced for soil (grain size, relative density, saturation) and model pile properties. The core analysis will use a factorial design approach, systematically varying one potential error source at a time in a high-fidelity numerical model (e.g., OpenSees or FLAC) calibrated with high-quality single-facility data. This virtual experimentation will isolate the sensitivity of key outputs (bending moment, downdrag load) to specific facility-dependent parameters like radial boundary stiffness, base excitation waveform, and pore fluid viscosity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a ranked, quantitative framework identifying the most critical parameters causing inter-facility variability, directly informing future benchmarking exercises and international collaborations. It will yield best-practice guidelines for mitigating these differences, thereby increasing the reliability and defensibility of centrifuge data for calibrating constitutive models and validating design methods for pile foundations.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have noted variability, this proposal moves beyond documentation to a controlled, virtual sensitivity analysis that disentangles and ranks the complex, coupled sources of error, a systematic approach not previously applied to this specific problem. It leverages the researcher's direct experience with centrifuge modeling of pile-soil interaction and novel container design to target the most plausible mechanistic causes.

proposals.title: A Verification and Validation Framework for Geotechnical Centrifuge Modeling: Benchmarking Facility Performance for Pile-Soil Interaction Studies
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a critical tool for validating numerical models and assessing complex soil-structure interaction, such as pile downdrag in liquefiable soils. However, the reliability of these physical experiments depends on rigorous, standardized assessment of facility performance, which is currently lacking. Adopting formal verification and validation (V&V) protocols from computational physics can provide the objective, quantitative benchmarks needed to establish confidence in centrifuge test results and enable meaningful model-to-model comparisons.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of a structured V&V framework, using well-defined benchmark problems with analytical or high-fidelity numerical solutions, will quantitatively reveal systematic biases and uncertainties in centrifuge facility performance related to boundary conditions, scaling laws, and instrumentation. This will allow for the calibration and improvement of experimental protocols.
proposals.method: The research will adapt the ASME V&V 20-2009 standard for centrifuge modeling. First, we will define a suite of verification benchmarks (e.g., elastic settlement of a rigid footing on a layered deposit) with closed-form solutions to test the accuracy of the centrifuge's scaling laws and measurement systems. Second, validation benchmarks will be established using high-quality numerical simulations (e.g., advanced constitutive models in FLAC or PLAXIS) of complex phenomena like liquefaction-induced pile downdrag. These benchmarks will be executed in the centrifuge using the novel instrumented container from recent fly-ash studies, with results compared quantitatively using metrics like the Validation Metric from the V&V guide.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formally defined V&V protocol for geotechnical centrifuge testing, providing a transparent methodology for assessing and reporting facility performance. It will deliver a publicly available suite of benchmark problems for pile-soil interaction, enabling cross-facility comparisons and strengthening the role of physical modeling in performance-based design. The outcome will be a significant step toward treating physical experiments with the same rigor as computational models.
proposals.novelty: While V&V is standard in computational physics, its systematic application to physical geotechnical modeling is novel. This approach moves beyond qualitative comparisons to provide quantitative, repeatable benchmarks for centrifuge performance, addressing a fundamental gap in experimental geotechnics.

proposals.title: Field-Scale Validation of Centrifuge Scaling Laws for Pile Downdrag and Slope Run-out in Geotechnical Fly Ash Deposits
proposals.motivation: Centrifuge modeling is a cornerstone of geotechnical engineering for simulating complex soil-structure interaction phenomena like liquefaction-induced downdrag on piles and flow failure run-out. However, the scaling laws governing these processes, particularly for non-standard materials like fly ash, require rigorous validation at prototype scale to ensure their predictive reliability for critical infrastructure design. Direct field-scale data for these failure modes in controlled, instrumented conditions is scarce, creating a gap between physical model predictions and real-world performance.
proposals.hypothesis: The scaling laws derived from centrifuge modeling of pile downdrag and slope run-out in fly ash deposits are directly applicable to field-scale behavior, and the mechanisms of deformation and load transfer observed in the centrifuge will be replicated at full scale with quantifiable fidelity.
proposals.method: This research will design and conduct two parallel, fully instrumented field tests at a suitable fly ash disposal site. The first test will involve installing instrumented piles within a saturated, loose fly ash deposit and inducing liquefaction via in-situ techniques (e.g., blast-induced or controlled water injection) to measure downdrag loads and pore pressure dissipation. The second test will construct a confined fly ash slope, instrument it with pore pressure transducers, inclinometers, and surface markers, and trigger a loss of confinement to measure run-out dynamics. Data on soil stresses, displacements, and pore pressures will be directly compared to equivalent centrifuge model tests (at 50-100g) conducted using the researcher's established apparatus, applying standard scaling relationships for time, stress, and velocity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rare, high-quality dataset directly validating centrifuge scaling laws for two critical failure modes. It will enhance confidence in using centrifuge modeling for the design of foundations and containment systems in fly ash and similar geotechnical materials. The findings will lead to refined guidelines for interpreting centrifuge test results for field-scale performance prediction.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuge tests on these phenomena exist, a controlled, side-by-side field validation targeting both pile downdrag and flow slide run-out in instrumented fly ash deposits has not been systematically attempted. This dual-phenomenon approach within a single material system provides a comprehensive test of scaling principles.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modeling of Pile-Soil Interaction and Settlement in Low-Gravity Regolith Simulants at Ultra-Low Effective Stresses
proposals.motivation: Understanding soil-structure interaction at very low effective stresses is critical for the design of foundations on the Moon and Mars, where gravity is a fraction of Earth's. On Earth, near-surface geotechnical problems, such as the behavior of very shallow foundations or lightweight structures on soft soils, also involve low stress regimes where classical constitutive models may not hold. Pre-2026 centrifuge modeling typically scales up stresses; this work inverts the paradigm to physically simulate extraterrestrial and extreme terrestrial conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The mobilization of shaft friction and the development of downdrag or settlement in piles within cohesionless soils undergo a fundamental shift at ultra-low effective stresses (below ~1 kPa), deviating significantly from behaviors predicted by conventional high-stress scaling laws and critical state soil mechanics.
proposals.method: The research will employ a geotechnical centrifuge to create a low-gravity environment, scaling down Earth's gravity to simulate lunar (0.166g) or Martian (0.38g) conditions. A novel, instrumented container will hold a desaturated, fine-grained soil (e.g., lunar regolith simulant JSC-1A or a fine silica flour). Instrumented model piles will be installed and subjected to axial loading or adjacent soil settlement (simulating liquefaction-induced downdrag protocols from prior work). Key measurements include pile load-displacement, local shaft stress via tactile sensors, and soil displacement via PIV, all at prototype effective stress levels as low as 0.5 kPa.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unique dataset on pile capacity and soil-structure interaction mechanics at previously untested stress levels. It will provide empirical validation or necessary modification factors for analytical and numerical models used in extraterrestrial foundation design. The findings will also inform the analysis of terrestrial structures on extremely weak or shallow soil deposits.
proposals.novelty: While centrifuges are standard for high-stress modeling, their use as a tool to *directly* achieve and test at prototype-scale ultra-low effective stresses for planetary geotechnics is novel. The experimental protocol merges Kutter's expertise in centrifuge modeling of pile downdrag and soil flow with the new challenge of low-gravity, low-stress soil mechanics.

ideas.title: Validating Foundation Models via Instrumented Field Pilots with Dimensional Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the validation paradigm from aerodynamics, where wind tunnel models are scaled up to full aircraft. The analogous problem is predicting full-scale system performance from physical models, solved through dimensional analysis and instrumented prototype testing. The adapted idea involves first establishing rigorous scaling laws (via dimensional analysis) for foundation behavior based on the existing database of physical model tests. Subsequently, a series of fully instrumented, full-scale foundation field pilots (e.g., test piles, small footings) are constructed under controlled, well-characterized soil conditions. These field prototypes are subjected to monitored loading. The core validation step is the direct, quantitative comparison of the measured field performance against the performance predicted by applying the scaling laws to the physical model data. This process validates the database trends for real-world scaling and pinpoints any discrepancies due to scale effects not replicated in the lab.

ideas.title: Calibration-Enabled Global Soil Property Inversion via Standardized CPT Rocking Response
ideas.description: This research fuses device calibration with inverse analysis to develop a novel framework for determining in-situ soil properties. It establishes rigorous, standardized protocols to calibrate miniature Cone Penetration Test (CPT) devices across labs, specifically quantifying their dynamic rocking response characteristics. These calibrated response 'fingerprints' are then used to pre-process field data from a shared global database, effectively normalizing measurements to a virtual standard device before inverse analysis. This enables the development of a universally reliable and accurate inverse solution that back-calculates soil strength and stiffness by isolating true soil behavior from instrument-specific artifacts, significantly improving cross-site comparisons and model reliability.

ideas.title: Applying a Dimensional Analysis Framework from Fluid Mechanics to Validate Constant Rotation for Moment Capacity
ideas.description: This research adapts the dimensional analysis framework from fluid mechanics to investigate the observed constant rotation for mobilizing moment capacity in geotechnical systems. The analogous solution is the use of the Buckingham π theorem to define dimensionless parameters that ensure dynamic similitude. The adapted idea is to apply this theorem to define the governing dimensionless groups for pile-soil moment interaction (e.g., incorporating rotation, soil modulus, strength, pile geometry, and loading eccentricity). The core investigation would then involve physical and numerical testing across a wide array of soil and geometry conditions to determine the specific combination of these dimensionless parameters for which the 0.01 rad rotation remains a valid criterion, thereby defining the limits of its applicability.

ideas.title: Applying Geophysical Imaging to Map and Model Heterogeneous Soil Profiles in Physical Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of soil homogeneity in physical models by adapting non-destructive imaging techniques from medical and materials science. The analogous solution uses wave propagation and sensor arrays to map internal heterogeneities, as seen in CT scans or ultrasound tomography. Adapted to geotechnical research, a physical model (e.g., a centrifuge or large-scale test chamber) would be equipped with a perimeter array of transducers and receivers. By analyzing the attenuation and velocity of seismic or electromagnetic waves sent through the soil volume before and during testing, a high-resolution 2D or 3D map of the actual soil layering, density variations, and moisture pockets can be reconstructed. This provides an empirical, spatially explicit soil profile to directly validate or challenge both the setup of uniform physical models and the predictions of numerical simulations that rely on simplified, homogeneous soil assumptions.

ideas.title: Nano-scale interrogation of extreme soil-footing interfaces to challenge the constant rotation paradigm
ideas.description: This research combines the macro-scale challenge to the constant rotation assumption with nano-indentation techniques to probe the fundamental mechanics at the soil-footing interface. By performing controlled nano-indentation and micro-scratching experiments on extreme soil types (e.g., highly crushable pumice, cemented sands, sensitive clays) and against materials/textures mimicking different footing shapes, it directly measures the initiation of interface failure and moment mobilization at the particle and fabric scale. This approach reveals whether the constant rotation assumption holds as a robust emergent property from diverse micro-mechanisms or if it breaks down due to specific interface-scale phenomena, providing a physically grounded basis to refine or challenge the macro-scale model.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Fusion of Rocking Response and Direct Site Investigation for Constrained Soil Property Estimation
ideas.description: This approach reframes the ill-posed inverse problem by using the measured rocking response not as the sole data source, but as a secondary constraint within a unified probabilistic soil model. A prior distribution of soil properties is first established using direct site investigation data, such as cone penetration tests and shear wave velocity profiles. The system then uses Bayesian updating to adjust this prior model, with the likelihood function evaluating how well the prior's predicted rocking response matches the field measurement, thereby reducing uncertainty in the final posterior estimates of strength and stiffness parameters.

ideas.title: Adapt Inter-Laboratory Comparison Protocols from Clinical Chemistry for Miniature CPT Calibration
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the well-established inter-laboratory proficiency testing (PT) model from clinical chemistry to solve the calibration and verification challenge for shared miniature Cone Penetration Tests (CPTs). In clinical labs, PT programs use centrally distributed reference samples and standardized analysis protocols to benchmark performance across hundreds of independent laboratories, identifying outliers and driving global harmonization. The adapted research idea involves creating a formal, recurring inter-laboratory comparison (ILC) program specifically for miniature CPTs. A central organization would develop geotechnically relevant, homogeneous, and stable soil reference samples with thoroughly characterized properties. Along with detailed, step-by-step calibration and testing protocols, these samples would be sent to participating geotechnical laboratories and operators. Each participant would conduct miniature CPT tests according to the protocol and submit their raw and processed data to a central database. The coordinating body would perform robust statistical analysis (e.g., using z-scores) to compare each lab's results against the assigned reference values and the peer group distribution. The resulting reports would not only verify individual instrument calibration but also pinpoint systematic errors related to specific operators, equipment setups, or data processing techniques, thereby providing a powerful tool for achieving true standardization across different research groups.

ideas.title: Adopt Cardiac Electrophysiology's Patient-Specific Heart Model Calibration for Rocking Foundation Digital Twins
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from cardiac electrophysiology, where patient-specific digital heart models are calibrated in real-time against live patient data. The analogous problem is creating a predictive, calibrated model of a complex, dynamic system. The adapted research idea is to create digital twins of rocking foundations that are not just static replicas but are dynamically calibrated models. The 'anatomy' of the foundation and soil strata is defined from site investigations, forming the base model governed by geotechnical principles. This model is then continuously calibrated in real-time using a stream of sensor data (e.g., from tiltmeters, accelerometers, settlement gauges) from the physical foundation, much like a heart model is calibrated against a live ECG. The goal is to iteratively adjust the twin's internal parameters (soil-structure interaction properties, damping, etc.) so its simulated performance matches the measured response, transforming it from a model based on general database trends into a high-fidelity, foundation-specific tool for real-time performance assessment and predictive maintenance.

ideas.title: Quantitatively Correlating Characterized Spatial Soil Variability with Physical Model Response
ideas.description: This research establishes a closed-loop framework where the spatial variability in a physical model is not merely introduced but first rigorously quantified. A constructed soil profile is characterized using embedded sensor arrays and geostatistical analysis to create a high-resolution digital model of its property fields (e.g., density, stiffness). This quantitatively defined heterogeneity then serves as the explicit input condition for subsequent physical loading tests (e.g., footing, slope, penetration). The output is a demonstrable, predictive relationship between specific statistical descriptors of the soil's inherent variability and key performance metrics of the geotechnical system, moving beyond qualitative challenge to quantitative, transferable insight.

ideas.title: Adapting Fatigue Testing from Materials Science: Require Dynamic Inertial Modeling for Seismic Assessment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption in seismic design that slow-cyclic loading data is sufficient for predicting structural performance under earthquakes. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where the insufficiency of low-frequency fatigue tests for high-dynamic environments was addressed, we adapt that solution. In materials fatigue, the field moved beyond S-N curves by developing tests and models that incorporate inertial effects and strain-rate sensitivity. Adapted to seismic engineering, this means rejecting the sufficiency of quasi-static cyclic tests. The research idea is to establish a new requirement that credible seismic performance assessment must model dynamic inertial forces and material rate effects explicitly. This would drive the development of next-generation hybrid simulation and testing frameworks that apply realistic, inertial-inclusive dynamic loading, fundamentally shifting the validation paradigm for seismic design codes.

ideas.title: Rocking of Deeply Embedded Foundations: A Hydrodynamic Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from naval hydrodynamics to study the dual problem of deeply embedded foundations rocking within the soil mass. The analogous problem—calculating the hydrodynamic coefficients for a deeply submerged body (like a submarine fin) oscillating in an infinite fluid—is solved using potential flow and boundary element methods. By modeling soil as a linear-elastic continuum instead of an ideal fluid, we adapt this approach using elastodynamic theory. The adapted idea is to develop a boundary element formulation where the embedded foundation is treated as a rigid inclusion undergoing small rocking oscillations. The solution will characterize the complex dynamic impedance (incorporating frequency-dependent stiffness and radiation damping) for the rocking mode, explicitly accounting for the effects of complete embedment and the three-dimensional radiation of stress waves into the surrounding soil medium, thereby providing a foundational solution for the analysis of deeply embedded structures like nuclear facility foundations or offshore monopiles under dynamic loads.

ideas.title: Predictive Scaling via Instrumented Intermediate Prototypes and Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This approach mandates a staged validation pathway between laboratory models and full-scale deployment. It employs heavily instrumented, intermediate-scale field prototypes (e.g., a single full-scale pile or a small raft foundation) to gather high-fidelity performance data under real site conditions. This data is continuously fed back to update and calibrate high-resolution numerical models and scaling laws. Only after the predictive models successfully simulate the intermediate prototype's behavior across key metrics are they used to design and justify a minimized, targeted campaign of full-scale tests, thereby de-risking the final validation step and ensuring the database trends are robustly scalable.

ideas.title: Adapting Statistical Physics of Glassy Systems to Model Micro-Scale Granular Rearrangements
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from the statistical physics of glassy systems to the problem of micro-scale granular mechanics. In glass physics, macroscopic viscous flow is explained by modeling microscopic particle 'cages' and collective rearrangement events. The adapted idea applies this framework to soil under rocking, treating it as a driven, athermal system near jamming. By using experimental techniques to track particle-scale movements and applying statistical analysis of local shear transformations and rearrangement avalanches, the research aims to build a predictive model that directly connects these micro-scale dynamics to the macro-scale database trends for energy dissipation and foundation settlement.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Hybrid ML for Robust Foundation Rocking Prediction
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid modeling framework that embeds a simplified physics-based rocking equation as a foundational layer within a machine learning architecture. The neural network learns to predict the discrepancy between high-fidelity simulation data and the simplified analytical model, effectively capturing complex, data-driven corrections while being inherently constrained by physical laws. This approach ensures predictions remain physically plausible even for novel input combinations, significantly improving extrapolation reliability. The resulting model provides both accurate performance predictions and interpretable insights into the sources of error in the classical analytical solution.

ideas.title: Meta-Analytic Predictive Models for Foundation Response with Quantified Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This research direction integrates meta-analysis techniques directly into the predictive model development process. Instead of using the compiled database as a simple training set, a formal meta-analysis will first be conducted to statistically synthesize results from diverse studies, quantifying between-study heterogeneity (e.g., due to soil type, scale, or loading procedure) as a core model parameter. This quantified heterogeneity is then used to inform and constrain the structure of new empirical or semi-empirical models, ensuring their predictions are accompanied by confidence intervals that reflect the true scatter in the underlying experimental evidence. The outcome is a new class of 'heterogeneity-aware' predictive models that are more robust and transparent for reliability-based design.

ideas.title: Predict Foundation Rocking via a Materials-Inspired Graph Neural Network
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry—using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to predict material properties from atomic structure—to the problem of predicting foundation rocking response. In the analogous field, GNNs excel by learning from the relational graph representation of a system. Adapted here, the foundation, soil, and structure are represented as a graph, with nodes for components (e.g., footing, columns, soil layers) and edges for their physical connections/interactions. This GNN model would be trained on a comprehensive database of rocking case histories or simulations, enabling it to learn the complex, non-linear mapping from the system's topological features and input parameters (e.g., geometry, load details, soil properties) directly to critical rocking performance outputs, offering a potentially more physics-aware and generalizable prediction tool than traditional ML on tabular data.

ideas.title: A Bias-Correcting Digital Twin and Reference Material Framework for Miniature CPT Inter-Laboratory Traceability
ideas.description: This project establishes a robust system to achieve true comparability of miniature CPT data across diverse laboratories, overcoming inherent environmental and operator variability. It develops a library of synthetic, geomechanically stable reference materials to serve as transfer standards, providing a common basis for measurement. A centralized digital twin ingests test data from all participating labs on both reference materials and natural samples, using machine learning to model and output lab-specific bias correction functions. This creates a traceable chain where any lab's data can be transformed to a virtual standard reference condition, enabling reliable data fusion and comparison despite differences in local practice and conditions.

ideas.title: Dual-probe system combining robust penetration profiling with targeted, validated pore pressure measurement
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a tandem tool string featuring a primary robust 6mm penetrometer for fast stratigraphic logging and tip resistance measurement. A secondary, dedicated miniature pore pressure probe, with an onboard saturation system and high-response filter, is advanced independently at a controlled rate. The system uses data from the primary probe to identify depths of interest (e.g., loose silt layers) and then activates the secondary probe to perform a proper, quasi-static penetration and pore pressure dissipation test at that specific target horizon. This ensures the pore pressure data meets CPTu validity standards without sacrificing the deployment speed and robustness of the primary penetrometer for general profiling.

ideas.title: Nano-indentation mapping of soil interface failure analogous to cartilage-bone delamination studies
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from biomechanics, where nano-indentation mapping is used to study failure at graded biological interfaces like cartilage-bone. The analogous problem of understanding delamination under complex loading is solved by spatially mapping mechanical properties across the interface to identify weak zones and failure mechanisms. Adapted to geomechanics, we propose using nano-indentation techniques to create detailed spatial maps of elastic modulus, hardness, and energy dissipation across the soil-footing interface zone. This will reveal the microscale weak layers, particle rearrangement, and localized failure initiation that govern the fundamental behavior during the initial mobilization of a rocking footing, moving beyond bulk interface measurements to a mechanistic, property-graded understanding.

ideas.title: Apply Environmental Risk Assessment Meta-Analysis to Experimental Synthesis
ideas.description: The original idea proposed applying medical meta-analysis to synthesize experimental studies. By analogy, the field of environmental science faces a similar challenge: synthesizing highly heterogeneous studies (lab, field, model) to assess chemical risks. Their solution uses hierarchical meta-analysis and weight-of-evidence frameworks to quantify and partition uncertainty. Adapted to materials science, this involves creating a hierarchical model for the experimental database that weights studies based on design quality and explicitly models variance from different synthesis methods, characterization tools, and sample conditions. The output is a robust, probabilistic synthesis of material properties with clear identification of the key experimental factors driving uncertainty, guiding future research toward standardizing the most critical variables.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery and Robustness Analysis for Geotechnical Performance Drivers
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple correlation mining by applying causal discovery algorithms to the geotechnical database to distinguish likely causal relationships from spurious associations. The system constructs a preliminary causal graph using background knowledge and constraint-based methods to model the complex web of influences between parameters and performance metrics. It then performs rigorous robustness checks, including sensitivity to unmeasured confounding, on the identified potential causal links. This approach yields more reliable, mechanistically interpretable insights into the true drivers of foundation performance, directly informing design and analysis practices.

ideas.title: Risk-informed dynamic analysis requirement framework for seismic design
ideas.description: This research develops a calibrated framework to replace the blanket assumption of slow-cyclic insufficiency. It establishes objective, performance-based criteria to determine when dynamic inertial effects must be explicitly modeled versus when equivalent static or cyclic procedures are adequate. The framework integrates structural dynamics principles with empirical data from past earthquakes and advanced simulations to create a transparent decision logic. This ensures rigorous analysis where truly needed while avoiding unnecessary computational burden for designs where conventional methods are proven reliable.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Physics-Constrained Digital Twins with Novelty Detection for Robust Foundation Monitoring
ideas.description: This research develops a resilient digital twin framework for rocking foundations that directly addresses the fragility of purely data-calibrated models. The system combines a baseline model informed by historical trends with a real-time Bayesian updating loop that continuously assimilates live sensor data to reduce uncertainty. Its core innovation is a hybrid modeling layer with automated novelty detection; when live performance deviates beyond a learned threshold, the system seamlessly transitions to rely on high-fidelity physics-based simulations, preventing model collapse. This ensures the digital twin remains a reliable monitoring and prediction tool even under novel, unforeseen loading scenarios not present in the original training database.

ideas.title: Dynamic Bayesian Meta-Analysis for Real-Time Geotechnical Health Monitoring
ideas.description: This research applies meta-analysis techniques to statistically synthesize the heterogeneous historical database of foundation performance studies, creating prior probability distributions for key geotechnical parameters and failure modes. These priors are then integrated into a dynamic Bayesian data fusion framework that continuously assimilates real-time sensor data from instrumented foundations. The synergy enables the system to not only detect anomalies in real-time but also quantify the statistical significance and confidence of each detection by updating the meta-analytic priors with the live data stream. This results in a continuously learning health monitoring system where the uncertainty bounds for both the historical knowledge and the current structural state are explicitly modeled and refined.

ideas.title: Develop predictive models for foundation behavior using a data-driven, ensemble learning approach adapted from quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the proven methodology from Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modeling in computational chemistry to the problem of predicting foundation settlement and rotation. In QSAR, a molecule's complex structure is reduced to numerical descriptors which are then correlated with its biological activity using advanced statistical and machine learning techniques. Similarly, each case history in the FoRCy database will be encoded into a comprehensive set of geotechnical and geometrical descriptors. After rigorous feature selection to reduce dimensionality and identify key drivers, ensemble learning algorithms (such as Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) will be trained to develop robust, data-driven predictive models. This approach aims to capture the intricate, non-linear relationships inherent in soil-structure interaction more effectively than traditional empirical methods, leading to models with improved predictive accuracy and generalizability for new foundation designs.

ideas.title: Unsupervised Feature Discovery for Geotechnical Performance Using Dimensionality Reduction and Clustering
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from bioinformatics to the geotechnical database context. In genomics, high-dimensional data is analyzed using unsupervised methods like PCA and t-SNE to discover latent patterns later correlated with outcomes. Similarly, we propose applying unsupervised machine learning—specifically dimensionality reduction and clustering techniques—to the entire array of variables in a geotechnical database beyond the standard settlement, rotation, and factor of safety. This process will create new synthetic features or identify natural clusters within the data. These derived features and clusters can then be rigorously correlated with performance metrics to uncover previously unrecognized, complex relationships and predictive parameter sets, moving beyond simple one-to-one correlations.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop, Field-Validated Digital Twins for Foundation Health Prognostics
ideas.description: This research direction establishes a closed-loop framework where data fusion techniques from structural health monitoring are deployed on instrumented, full-scale field test foundations. The real-time sensor streams are continuously assimilated with historical and model-derived database knowledge to detect anomalies and predict performance degradation. Crucially, the outcomes of these full-scale tests provide ground-truth validation, which is fed back to dynamically update and improve the accuracy of the fused data models. This creates a continuously learning digital twin system capable of reliable prognostics for foundation health management.

ideas.title: Cluster-Discovered Bayesian Meta-Analysis for Heterogeneous Materials Data
ideas.description: This system first employs unsupervised machine learning on study descriptors and outcomes to automatically identify coherent clusters of comparable experiments, preventing invalid synthesis across fundamentally different phenomena. Within each validated cluster, it applies Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis models to quantify the central tendency and uncertainty of material properties or performance metrics. This approach provides rigorous, cluster-specific quantitative summaries while transparently reporting the heterogeneity and comparability limits that define each analysis. The result is a robust, data-driven synthesis framework tailored for the inherent diversity of materials science data.

ideas.title: Robust Anomaly Detection Under Sensor Degradation via Probabilistic Digital Twin Assimilation
ideas.description: This research designs a structural health monitoring system that remains robust despite inevitable sensor network failures. A probabilistic digital twin, continuously updated via ensemble-based data assimilation, infers the complete structural state from sparse and intermittent real-time data fused with historical models. The system includes a diagnostic module to detect and isolate faulty sensors, allowing the fusion engine to dynamically reconfigure and maintain reliable anomaly detection using only validated data streams. This approach ensures continuous monitoring integrity even under significant data loss or degradation.
